_meta.description: Complete diagnostic rule engine from Workshop Diagnostic. Rules are evaluated against live PID data to generate actionable diagnostic findings with severity levels and repair guidance.
_meta.language: TypeScript (transcribed to JSON for V5 use)

logic_types.ALL: All conditions must be met (logical AND)
logic_types.ANY: At least one condition must be met (logical OR)

repair_priority.4: Low — monitor, repair at next service

rules.name: Lean Condition Bank 1
rules.description: Bank 1 running lean — ECU compensating with high positive fuel trims.
rules.recommendation: Positive fuel trims indicate lean condition. Check for vacuum leaks (use intake cleaner or smoke machine), MAF sensor contamination, low fuel pressure, or clogged fuel injectors.
rules.technicalNotes: Combined STFT+LTFT > 20% makes vacuum leak more likely. Lean condition at idle only suggests vacuum leak. Lean at all RPM suggests MAF or fuel delivery issue.

rules.name: Severe Lean Condition
rules.description: Both banks running severely lean — multiple system involvement likely.
rules.recommendation: Both banks at trim limits — severe lean condition. Causes: Large vacuum leak, MAF failure, fuel pump failure, blocked fuel filter. Risk of engine damage from detonation. Address immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: At trim limits (>25%) engine may miss, stall, or suffer detonation damage. Check fuel pressure before smoke testing.

rules.name: Lean at Idle Only
rules.description: Lean condition present only at idle, normalizes at higher RPM — suggests vacuum leak.
rules.recommendation: Lean at idle with normal trims at cruise is highly characteristic of vacuum leak. Small leaks have greater effect at low manifold pressure (high vacuum) at idle. Use smoke machine or propane enrichment to locate leak.
rules.technicalNotes: Small vacuum leaks add proportionally more air at idle (when throttle is mostly closed and manifold vacuum is highest) than at WOT. Classic diagnostic: trim normalizes above 2000 RPM = vacuum leak.

rules.name: Rich Condition Bank 1
rules.description: Bank 1 running rich — ECU compensating with negative fuel trims.
rules.recommendation: Negative fuel trims indicate rich condition. Check: Leaking fuel injectors, high fuel pressure, saturated EVAP canister, coolant temp sensor reading cold, MAF sensor over-reading.
rules.technicalNotes: Rich condition causes catalyst damage and poor fuel economy. Long-term rich = oil dilution from fuel wash-down.

rules.name: Severe Rich Condition
rules.description: Both banks at negative trim limits — significant over-fueling.
rules.recommendation: Severe rich condition. Possible causes: Failed MAF (sky-high reading), multiple leaking injectors, ruptured fuel pressure regulator diaphragm, very high fuel pressure. Risk of catalytic converter damage and oil dilution.
rules.technicalNotes: Disconnect MAF and see if symptoms improve. If yes, MAF over-reading is cause.

rules.name: Rich After Cold Start
rules.description: Excessive richness during warm-up — cold start enrichment not closing off.
rules.recommendation: Excessive enrichment during warm-up. Check ECT sensor accuracy (may be reading too cold), cold-start injector (if equipped), or injector leak. Rich cold start causes excess HC emissions and catalyst contamination.
rules.technicalNotes: ECT sensor wrong = ECU thinks engine is always cold, keeps enriching. Check resistance vs temperature chart.

rules.name: Bank Imbalance — Bank 2 Lean
rules.description: Significant difference between bank 1 and bank 2 fuel trims (Bank 2 leaner).
rules.recommendation: Bank-specific lean condition points to bank-side issue: Intake manifold gasket leak on Bank 2, exhaust manifold crack before Bank 2 O2 sensor, or clogged Bank 2 injectors.
rules.technicalNotes: V-engine bank issues are confirmed when trim difference exceeds 5-7%. Injector balance test on both banks can isolate injector issues.

rules.name: Engine Overheating
rules.description: Coolant temperature in danger zone — risk of severe engine damage.
rules.recommendation: STOP IMMEDIATELY. Pull over, engine off. Do not open radiator cap when hot. Common causes: Loss of coolant (leak), cooling fan failure, head gasket, water pump failure, blocked radiator. Do not drive overheating vehicle.
rules.estimatedRepairTime: Varies significantly — could be head rebuild
rules.technicalNotes: Single overheating event above 130°C can warp aluminum heads. Check for white exhaust smoke (coolant burning) and sweet smell.

rules.name: Engine Running Too Cool
rules.description: Engine not reaching normal operating temperature — thermostat likely stuck open.

conditions.description: Coolant temp below normal after warm-up

rules.recommendation: Thermostat likely stuck open causing excessive cooling. Engine runs over-rich, poor fuel economy, increased wear. Replace thermostat — inexpensive fix with significant benefit.
rules.technicalNotes: P0128 DTC (coolant temperature below thermostat regulating temp) is the classic code for stuck-open thermostat.

rules.name: Rapid Coolant Temperature Rise
rules.description: Coolant temperature rising unusually fast — potential coolant loss or circulation issue.
rules.recommendation: Rapid temperature rise indicates coolant loss or circulation failure. Pull over safely. Common causes: Major coolant leak, water pump failure, head gasket blown. Check for white steam from exhaust.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal warm-up rate is 0.2-0.5°C/sec. Above 2°C/sec at operating temp is catastrophic-rate rise.

rules.name: Lazy O2 Sensor
rules.description: Oxygen sensor response time too slow — ECU fuel control degraded.
rules.recommendation: O2 sensor response slow or stuck (lazy sensor). ECU cannot properly control fuel mixture. Replace O2 sensor. Lazy sensors are common after 100,000 km.
rules.technicalNotes: Healthy O2 switches 3-8 times/sec in closed loop. Lazy sensor switches < 1/sec causing poor fuel economy and emissions.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Lean
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor persistently reading lean — causing over-fueling correction.
rules.recommendation: O2 sensor reading lean continuously. Could be: Genuine severe lean condition, exhaust leak before sensor diluting reading, or failed sensor. Rule out exhaust leak before replacing sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Exhaust leak ahead of O2 sensor is the classic reason for stuck-lean despite rich fuel trims. Fuel trim will be very positive trying to compensate for false lean reading.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Stuck Rich
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor persistently reading rich.
rules.recommendation: O2 persistently high. Genuine rich condition with ECU trimming fuel negative, or biased sensor. Compare with fuel trims: large negative LTFT + stuck-rich O2 = fuel delivery issue. Normal LTFT + stuck-rich O2 = sensor bias.
rules.technicalNotes: Contaminated sensor (silicone, RTV) can read falsely rich. Replace sensor if other causes eliminated.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Heater Failure
rules.description: O2 sensor not reaching operating temperature quickly — heater circuit failure.
rules.recommendation: O2 sensor heater likely failed. Sensor running cold causes open-loop operation and increased emissions. Check heater circuit fuse and ground. Replace sensor if wiring OK.
rules.technicalNotes: Heated O2 reaches operating temp (~350°C) in 10-30 seconds. Without heater, may take 2+ minutes on cold days — causing extended open-loop operation.

rules.name: Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold
rules.description: Catalytic converter not effectively cleaning exhaust — emissions test failure likely.
rules.recommendation: Catalyst failing emissions. Causes: Normal aging past 100,000 km, contamination from oil/coolant burn, overheating from misfires, fuel additive damage. Check for P0420/P0430. Replace catalyst after fixing root cause.
rules.technicalNotes: Always diagnose what killed the catalyst (oil burning, misfire, coolant) before replacement, or new cat will fail too.

rules.name: Catalytic Converter Overheating
rules.description: Catalyst temperature dangerously high — risk of substrate meltdown.
rules.recommendation: CRITICAL — Catalyst overheating. Most common cause: Active misfire pushing raw fuel into catalyst (fuel burns inside cat). Stop engine, diagnose misfire immediately. Sustained over-temp melts substrate, can cause underbody fire.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal cat operating temp: 400-800°C. A single misfire event can push it to 1000°C+. Fix misfire before assessing cat.

rules.name: MAF Sensor Contamination
rules.description: MAF sensor reading lower than expected — element contamination suspected.
rules.recommendation: MAF reading lower than expected with positive trims. Clean MAF with dedicated MAF cleaner spray. If trims improve after cleaning, MAF was contaminated. Replace if cleaning doesn't help.
rules.technicalNotes: Even a thin oil film on the hot-wire element reduces MAF reading significantly. Never touch MAF element or use generic cleaners.

rules.name: MAF Sensor Over-Reading
rules.description: MAF sensor reading higher than expected — causes over-fueling.
rules.recommendation: MAF reads high causing over-fueling. Check for: Air filter housing not sealed, MAF installed backwards, air leak after MAF measuring extra air counted by sensor. Disconnect MAF to force speed-density mode as test.
rules.technicalNotes: With MAF disconnected, ECU uses MAP/speed-density. If condition improves, MAF is over-reading.

rules.name: MAF to Load Correlation Failure
rules.description: MAF reading not matching calculated engine load — sensor or air system issue.
rules.recommendation: MAF and load signals disagree — ECU may be confused about actual airflow. Common causes: Air leak between MAF and throttle body (MAF reads more than engine receives), or load calculation issue.
rules.technicalNotes: A crack in the air hose between MAF and throttle body is a classic cause. MAF reads total airflow, but some leaks out before reaching cylinders.

rules.name: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Specific misfire detected on cylinder 1.
rules.recommendation: Cylinder 1 specific misfire. Swap coil/plug with adjacent cylinder to isolate. If misfire moves: Coil or plug at fault. If misfire stays: Injector, compression, or wiring specific to cyl 1.
rules.technicalNotes: Swap test: Move coil from cyl 1 to cyl 2. If code moves to P0302, coil is cause. If stays P0301, inject or compression.

rules.name: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfires
rules.description: Multiple cylinders misfiring or misfire count not cylinder-specific.
rules.recommendation: Random multi-cylinder misfires suggest systemic cause: Low fuel pressure (affects all cylinders), ignition module failure, vacuum leak, EGR stuck open, or low compression (failing rings/head gasket).
rules.technicalNotes: P0300 (random misfire) without a cylinder-specific code usually indicates fuel or compression issue rather than ignition.

rules.name: Misfire at Idle Only
rules.description: Misfires occurring only at idle, clearing at higher RPM.
rules.recommendation: Idle-only misfire suggests: EGR stuck open (diluting mixture at idle), dirty IAC causing lean idle misfire, weak coil losing spark at low RPM when spark energy is lowest, or EVAP purge active at idle.
rules.technicalNotes: Coils that work at higher RPM (faster dwell recovery) but miss at idle are often carbon-tracked or have weak coil towers.

rules.name: Misfire Under Load
rules.description: Misfires increasing with throttle/load — suggests fuel or compression issue.
rules.recommendation: Load-only misfires indicate: Fuel delivery not keeping up under demand (weak pump/clogged filter), ignition timing too retarded, or compression dropping under cylinder pressure. Check fuel pressure under load.
rules.technicalNotes: Misfires under load are particularly damaging to catalytic converters — raw fuel dumps straight in and ignites catalytically.

rules.name: High Idle RPM
rules.description: Engine idle speed above normal range.
rules.recommendation: High idle usually caused by: IAC valve stuck open, vacuum leak (unregulated air bypassing throttle), throttle plate not closing fully (cable binding or carbon), or ECU commanded high idle (A/C, DEF warmup complete but timer not resetting).
rules.technicalNotes: First ensure throttle closes fully mechanically. Then check IAC, then smoke test for vacuum leaks. ECU-commanded high idle is normal for A/C or cold start.

rules.name: Low Idle RPM
rules.description: Engine idle speed below normal range — risk of stalling.
rules.recommendation: Low idle risk of stall. Check: Carbon buildup on throttle plate (clean throttle body), IAC not supplying enough bypass air, fuel trim extremely lean causing low combustion pressure, or EGR stuck open diluting mixture.
rules.technicalNotes: After throttle body cleaning, may need to perform idle relearn procedure (let ECU relearn closed throttle position).

rules.name: Hunting/Unstable Idle
rules.description: RPM oscillating at idle — idle control unable to stabilize.
rules.recommendation: Hunting idle caused by: IAC controller oscillating, vacuum leak causing RPM to swing, EVAP purge dumping extra fuel creating rich/lean cycle, or TPS signal noise causing ECU confusion about throttle position.
rules.technicalNotes: Disconnect EVAP purge hose as a test. If idle stabilizes, EVAP purge is contributing to oscillation.

rules.name: TPS Signal Erratic
rules.description: Throttle position sensor showing erratic readings — affects multiple systems.
rules.recommendation: Erratic TPS signal affects ignition timing, fuel delivery, and transmission shift points. Check TPS wiring harness for chafing, inspect connector for corrosion, measure TPS resistance sweep. Replace TPS if carbon track issue.
rules.technicalNotes: Wiggle TPS connector while monitoring signal. Dropouts during wiggle = connector/wiring issue. Carbon track worn = intermittent dead spots in sensor sweep.

rules.name: TPS Not Returning to Zero
rules.description: Throttle position sensor not reading zero at idle.
rules.recommendation: TPS reading above 0 at idle. Could be: Physical throttle plate not closing (cable binding, carbon build-up), or TPS biased high (needs recalibration or replacement). Verify throttle closes mechanically before blaming sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: On DBW systems, TPS calibration is done in ECU. On cable throttle, physical adjustment is needed.

rules.name: EGR Valve Stuck Open
rules.description: EGR valve not closing, causing rough idle and stalling.

conditions.description: MAP higher than expected at idle (low vacuum)

rules.recommendation: EGR stuck open causes rough idle, stalling, and poor fuel economy. Carbon buildup is common cause. Remove and clean EGR valve, clean passages in intake manifold. Replace EGR if cleaning doesnt help.
rules.technicalNotes: Diesel EGR systems are particularly prone to carbon buildup. Use walnut shell blasting for intake cleaning.

rules.name: EGR Flow Insufficient
rules.description: EGR not flowing when commanded, increasing NOx emissions.
rules.recommendation: EGR not flowing adequately. Check: EGR passages clogged with carbon, vacuum supply to EGR actuator, EGR solenoid function, exhaust backpressure supply. Common on high-mileage vehicles.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR codes often fail emissions testing. EGR function required for compliance.

rules.name: EVAP System Large Leak Detected
rules.description: Significant leak in evaporative emission control system.
rules.recommendation: Large EVAP leak: Loose or missing gas cap (check first!), cracked EVAP hose, failed purge valve, damaged charcoal canister, or missing vent valve seal. Use smoke machine for diagnosis.
rules.technicalNotes: Always start by checking/replacing gas cap. It's free diagnosis and often fixes P0455.

rules.name: EVAP Purge Valve Stuck Open
rules.description: Purge valve not operating correctly, causing rich condition.
rules.recommendation: Purge valve stuck open causes: Rich running, rough idle when tank is full, strong fuel smell. Check purge valve holds vacuum when off, clicks when activated. Replace if stuck open.
rules.technicalNotes: Stuck-open purge valve dumps fuel vapor into engine constantly, causing rich condition especially at idle.

rules.name: Turbo Boost Leak Detected
rules.description: Boost pressure not reaching expected levels — pressure loss in charged intake.
rules.recommendation: Not reaching boost target: Boost leak in intercooler piping (most common), wastegate stuck open, turbo bearing failure, or boost control solenoid issue. Pressure test intercooler system first.
rules.technicalNotes: Boost leak can be found with soapy water while pressurizing intake system. Listen for hissing under load.

rules.name: Overboost Condition
rules.description: Boost pressure exceeding safe limits — engine damage risk.
rules.recommendation: DANGER — Overboost can damage engine. Possible causes: Wastegate stuck closed, boost control solenoid failed open, ECU boost limit bypassed. Vehicle may enter limp mode. Do not drive until fixed.
rules.technicalNotes: Overboost causes detonation, bent rods, blown head gaskets. Critical failure mode requiring immediate attention.

rules.name: Excessive Turbo Lag
rules.description: Turbocharger slow to spool up despite adequate RPM.
rules.recommendation: Turbo not spooling properly: Exhaust restriction (clogged cat/DPF), turbo bearing wear, VGT actuator stuck, exhaust leak before turbo, or boost leak reducing compressor loading.
rules.technicalNotes: Check for shaft play in turbo (should be minimal). Blue smoke indicates oil seal failure.

rules.name: Alternator Not Charging Adequately
rules.description: System voltage below normal while engine running.
rules.recommendation: Low charging voltage: Worn alternator, slipping belt, high resistance in charging circuit, or failing battery. Check belt tension, battery connections, and alternator output at alternator terminal.
rules.technicalNotes: Undercharging kills batteries and can leave you stranded. Check output at alternator terminal, not battery.

rules.name: Alternator Overcharging
rules.description: System voltage above safe limits — voltage regulator failure.
rules.recommendation: DANGER — Overcharging damages electrical components and can cause battery explosion. Likely voltage regulator failure internal to alternator. Replace alternator. Check for burnt electronics.
rules.technicalNotes: Over 16V can blow light bulbs, damage ECUs, and boil battery. Stop driving immediately.

rules.name: Low Oil Pressure at Idle
rules.description: Oil pressure below safe threshold at idle — engine damage imminent.
rules.recommendation: LOW OIL PRESSURE — ENGINE DAMAGE IMMINENT. Check oil level immediately. If level OK: Worn bearings, oil pump failure, clogged pickup screen, wrong viscosity. Stop driving until diagnosed.
rules.technicalNotes: Low oil pressure warning should never be ignored. Even brief low pressure episodes cause bearing damage.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Not Rising with RPM
rules.description: Oil pressure staying low as RPM increases — pump or bearing failure.
rules.recommendation: Oil pressure should increase significantly with RPM. No increase indicates: Worn oil pump, stuck pressure relief valve, severely worn bearings, or oil bypass issue. Use mechanical gauge to verify before teardown.
rules.technicalNotes: Always verify with mechanical gauge. Electronic oil pressure sensors can fail, giving false readings.

rules.name: Transmission Overheating
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature exceeding safe limits.
rules.recommendation: STOP AND COOL DOWN. ATF breaks down rapidly above 120°C. Check: Fluid level, cooler blockage, torque converter lockup issues, slipping clutches. Towing or heavy load in hot weather is common cause.
rules.technicalNotes: Every 10°C over 90°C roughly halves ATF life. Overheated fluid loses lubrication — damage is cumulative.

rules.name: Transmission Slipping Detected
rules.description: RPM rising without corresponding vehicle speed increase — clutch slip.
rules.recommendation: Transmission slip detected. Check fluid level and condition first (burnt smell = damaged clutches). Low fluid, worn clutches, or failing torque converter. Service may help if caught early, rebuild if severe.
rules.technicalNotes: Slipping creates heat which causes more slipping — address promptly to avoid complete failure.

rules.name: DPF Regeneration Required
rules.description: Diesel Particulate Filter soot loading high — regeneration needed.
rules.recommendation: DPF needs regeneration. Drive on highway (sustained > 60 km/h for 15-20 minutes) to allow passive/active regen. Frequent short trips prevent natural regen. Forced regen may be needed if pattern doesn't allow.
rules.estimatedRepairTime: 20 min highway drive or 1 hour forced regen
rules.technicalNotes: Don't ignore regen warnings — eventually DPF will clog completely and require expensive replacement or professional cleaning.

rules.name: DPF Regeneration Failure
rules.description: DPF unable to regenerate successfully — critical soot accumulation.
rules.recommendation: DPF regen not completing. Causes: Regen interrupted by driving pattern, faulty injector (can't create post-injection heat), glow plug failure, temperature sensor issue. Forced regen with scan tool or DPF cleaning/replacement.
rules.technicalNotes: Multiple failed regens cause ash buildup that can't be burned off. Professional cleaning or replacement eventually needed.

rules.name: Open Loop When Warm
rules.description: Engine running in open-loop fuel control despite being at operating temperature — O2 feedback disabled.

conditions.description: Fuel system status NOT closed loop

rules.recommendation: Engine not entering closed-loop fuel control after warmup. Check O2 sensor heater fuse and circuit, verify O2 sensor resistance (should be 6-15Ω for heater), check for pending O2 heater DTCs (P0135, P0141).
rules.technicalNotes: Status byte 0x03: 1=open loop, 2=closed loop, 4=open loop drive, 8=open loop fault. Only status 2 or 6 indicate proper closed-loop operation.

rules.name: Lazy O2 Sensor
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor switching too slowly — degraded response time indicates aging sensor.
rules.recommendation: O2 sensor responding but switching slowly. Compare STFT oscillation speed — if STFT also oscillates slowly, sensor is likely lazy. Replace upstream O2 sensor. Note: this is a time-series diagnosis — confidence improves with longer scan duration.
rules.technicalNotes: A healthy NBO2 switches 3-8 times/second. Response time > 300ms per transition is considered lazy (should be < 100ms). TSM o2CrossingCount tracks this.

rules.name: Timing Retard Under Load
rules.description: Ignition timing excessively retarded under high load — knock sensor activity or detonation.
rules.recommendation: Significant timing retard under load indicates knock sensor detecting detonation. Check fuel quality (octane), carbon buildup on pistons/valves, coolant temperature, and EGR function. Hot spots from carbon deposits are a common cause.
rules.technicalNotes: Typical timing at 70%+ load: 10-20° BTDC depending on RPM. Below 5° indicates ECU is pulling significant timing to prevent knock. Sustained knock can damage pistons and bearings.

rules.name: MAP Indicates Vacuum Leak
rules.description: Manifold absolute pressure too high at idle — insufficient engine vacuum suggests air leak.
rules.recommendation: Idle manifold vacuum too low (pressure too high). Normal idle MAP is 25-45 kPa (15-22 inHg vacuum). High MAP at idle suggests vacuum leak, EGR stuck open, or significant timing retard. Smoke test intake system.
rules.technicalNotes: At sea level, barometric = ~101 kPa. Healthy idle vacuum creates 25-45 kPa MAP (56-76 kPa vacuum). Each 5 kPa above normal at idle ≈ a significant leak.

rules.name: DTCs Recently Cleared
rules.description: OBD monitors reset recently — possible underlying fault masked by code clearing.
rules.recommendation: DTCs were recently cleared. OBD readiness monitors have not completed their drive cycles yet. If this is after a repair, drive normally for 50-100 km to allow monitors to run. If codes return, the underlying issue persists.
rules.technicalNotes: Complete drive cycle requires: cold start, idle 2 min, accelerate to 60 km/h, cruise 5 min, decelerate, idle. Some monitors need specific conditions (e.g., EVAP needs specific tank level and temp).

rules.name: Extended MIL Operation
rules.description: Check Engine Light has been on for significant driving distance — potential secondary damage from unresolved fault.
rules.recommendation: Vehicle has been driven 500+ km with Check Engine Light on. Extended operation with an emissions fault risks catalyst damage, increased fuel consumption, and potentially more expensive repairs. Diagnose and repair the underlying cause.
rules.technicalNotes: P0420/P0430 (catalyst efficiency) is relatively benign if driving short-term. P030x misfire codes while driving long distances can melt catalytic converter substrate — repair urgently.

rules.name: Lean Condition at Cruise
rules.description: Lean fuel trims detected while cruising at steady speed — fuel delivery insufficient under sustained load.
rules.recommendation: Lean at cruise but not idle points to fuel delivery restriction rather than vacuum leak. Check fuel filter, fuel pump pressure under load, and fuel pressure regulator.
rules.technicalNotes: Unlike idle lean (vacuum leak), cruise lean occurs when fuel demand increases but supply can't keep up. Fuel pressure drop-off test under load is diagnostic.

rules.name: Lean at Wide Open Throttle
rules.description: Lean detected under heavy acceleration — risk of detonation and engine damage.
rules.recommendation: DANGER: Lean under high load causes detonation, piston damage, and head gasket failure. Avoid WOT driving. Check fuel pump capacity, clogged injectors, fuel pressure regulator, and MAF sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Lean at WOT is the most damaging scenario — pistons absorb heat that should go to burning fuel. Detonation follows rapidly. Fuel pressure under WOT should be within 10% of spec.

rules.name: Rich Condition at Idle
rules.description: Rich fuel mixture at idle — excessive fuel delivery when engine demand is minimal.
rules.recommendation: Rich at idle suggests leaking injector(s), fuel pressure regulator leak, or EVAP purge valve stuck open flooding intake. Check for fuel smell in oil (dilution risk).
rules.technicalNotes: Rich at idle fouls spark plugs, washes cylinder walls (oil dilution), and loads catalyst with unburned fuel (overheat risk).

rules.name: Extended Cold Start Enrichment
rules.description: Rich condition persisting beyond normal cold-start enrichment period (>3 minutes).
rules.recommendation: Cold start enrichment should taper off as engine warms. Persistent rich after 3 minutes suggests coolant temp sensor reading colder than actual, or open-loop mode prolonged. Verify ECT sensor accuracy.
rules.technicalNotes: ECU uses ECT to determine enrichment amount. A sensor reading 20°C when actual is 50°C causes continued enrichment, fouling plugs and wasting fuel.

rules.name: Fuel Trim Oscillating
rules.description: STFT rapidly swinging positive and negative — unstable mixture control.
rules.recommendation: Wide STFT oscillation indicates ECU hunting for correct mixture. Common causes: intermittent vacuum leak (heat-dependent), lazy O2 sensor, or intake manifold runner flap issue.
rules.technicalNotes: Stable STFT should be within ±5% at steady state. Oscillation > ±12% means ECU can't stabilize the mixture — sensor response or air/fuel input is erratic.

rules.name: Low Fuel Pressure
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure below normal operating range — insufficient fuel delivery.
rules.recommendation: Low fuel pressure causes lean condition and potential misfires under load. Check fuel pump output, fuel filter restriction, fuel pressure regulator, and check for kinked/restricted fuel lines.
rules.technicalNotes: For port injection: typical 270-310 kPa (40-45 psi). For GDI: 3000-15000 kPa. This rule targets port injection vehicles.

rules.name: High Fuel Pressure
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure above normal range — stuck regulator or restricted return line.
rules.recommendation: High fuel pressure causes rich mixture. Check fuel pressure regulator for stuck closed condition, pinched/blocked fuel return line, or wrong fuel pump installed.
rules.technicalNotes: High fuel pressure floods injectors — each pulse delivers more fuel than calibrated. Negative LTFT (ECU pulling fuel) is the expected compensation.

rules.name: STFT at Lean Trim Limit
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim at or near maximum positive authority — ECU cannot compensate further.
rules.recommendation: STFT at maximum authority means ECU is adding maximum possible fuel and mixture is STILL lean. This is a serious fuel delivery or air leak issue. Large vacuum leak, failed fuel pump, or completely clogged injector(s).
rules.technicalNotes: Trim limit is typically ±25-30%. When STFT hits ceiling, remaining correction falls to LTFT. When both max out, mixture goes uncontrolled.

rules.name: STFT at Rich Trim Limit
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim at or near maximum negative authority — ECU cannot pull enough fuel.
rules.recommendation: STFT at maximum negative authority — ECU pulling maximum fuel but mixture still rich. Leaking injector(s), very high fuel pressure, or coolant intrusion into combustion. Check for white exhaust smoke (coolant), fuel smell in oil, or fouled plugs.
rules.technicalNotes: Rich trim-limit with no external fuel smell may indicate coolant entering combustion chambers (head gasket or intake gasket leak).

rules.name: Combined Trim Excessive
rules.description: Sum of STFT + LTFT exceeding safe limits — total fuel correction is very high.
rules.recommendation: Combined STFT+LTFT > 15% means significant lean compensation. ECU has learned a base lean condition (LTFT) and is still correcting (STFT). Issue is ongoing and getting worse.
rules.technicalNotes: LTFT represents learned correction. STFT on top means the learned value isn't enough — the condition is worsening or variable.

rules.name: Combined Trim Excessive Rich
rules.description: Sum of STFT + LTFT deeply negative — both corrections pulling fuel.
rules.recommendation: Combined STFT+LTFT < -15% means significant rich compensation. ECU has learned a base rich condition and is still correcting. Leaking injector(s), stuck EVAP purge, or high fuel pressure.
rules.technicalNotes: Rich condition risks oil dilution (fuel washing cylinder walls), catalyst overheating, and elevated emissions.

rules.name: Low Fuel Level Impact
rules.description: Fuel level critically low — risk of fuel pump overheating and air ingestion into fuel lines.
rules.recommendation: Running with very low fuel causes fuel pump to run hotter (fuel provides cooling), and can draw air/sediment from tank bottom. Refuel soon to prevent pump damage.
rules.technicalNotes: Fuel pump submerged in fuel for cooling. Below 10% tank, pump exposed to air — accelerates pump motor wear. Sediment at tank bottom can also clog fuel filter.

rules.name: Cooling Fan Not Activating
rules.description: Coolant temperature rising above fan activation point but temp continues to climb.
rules.recommendation: ECT > 100°C while stationary/slow suggests cooling fan not engaging. Check fan relay, fan motor, coolant temp switch/sensor for fan, and fuse. Also check coolant level.
rules.technicalNotes: Fans typically activate at 95-102°C. In traffic (low speed, no airflow), fans are the only cooling mechanism. Failure leads to rapid overheating.

rules.name: Thermostat Stuck Open
rules.description: Engine temperature not reaching operating range — thermostat not closing to warm engine.
rules.recommendation: After 10 min of driving, ECT should be 85-95°C. Stuck-open thermostat allows constant coolant flow through radiator, preventing warmup. Increases fuel consumption 5-15%, accelerates wear.
rules.technicalNotes: In tropical climate, 10 min driving should achieve 85°C+. Below 80°C after extended driving is definitive thermostat failure.

rules.name: Thermal Soak at Idle
rules.description: Coolant temperature rising steadily while idling — heat buildup without adequate dissipation.
rules.recommendation: Temp rising while stationary is expected in tropical climates, but beyond 98°C the fan should prevent further rise. Monitor closely — if temp exceeds 105°C, stop engine. Check fan operation and coolant level.
rules.technicalNotes: Tropical ambient (30-38°C) reduces radiator ΔT. Lower efficiency is expected. Fan cycling between 95-102°C is normal.

rules.name: Head Gasket Suspect
rules.description: Coolant temp rising with lean trims — possible combustion gas intrusion into cooling system.
rules.recommendation: High coolant temp PLUS lean trims can indicate head gasket breach: combustion pressure enters cooling system (overheating) while coolant enters cylinder (lean). Check for: white exhaust smoke, oil/coolant mixing, bubbles in coolant reservoir, and hydrocarbon presence in coolant.
rules.technicalNotes: Head gasket breach between combustion chamber and water jacket. Compression test, leak-down test, and combustion gas test of coolant are definitive diagnostics.

rules.name: ECT Sensor Error (Reading Too High)
rules.description: Coolant temperature reading implausibly high — possible sensor short or wiring issue.
rules.recommendation: ECT above 130°C is rare even in extreme conditions — likely sensor or wiring fault. A shorted sensor reads high, causing ECU to reduce enrichment (lean) and run fans constantly. Verify with infrared thermometer.
rules.technicalNotes: NTC thermistor: low resistance = high reading. Short circuit reads very hot. Open circuit reads very cold (different failure mode).

rules.name: ECT Sensor Error (Reading Too Low)
rules.description: Coolant temperature reading implausibly low after extended runtime — sensor open circuit.
rules.recommendation: ECT below 20°C after 10 minutes of operation is physically impossible in any climate. Sensor has open-circuit fault, causing ECU to read maximum cold. This causes perpetual cold-start enrichment = rich running, high fuel consumption, fouled plugs.
rules.technicalNotes: NTC thermistor open circuit = high resistance = reads very cold. ECU assumes cold engine and runs extra enrichment indefinitely.

rules.name: IAT Sensor Reading Too High
rules.description: Intake air temperature reading implausibly high — affects fuel calculations and timing.
rules.recommendation: IAT above 70°C means sensor reading underhood radiant heat or has a fault. High IAT causes ECU to retard timing (knock protection) and lean out mixture. Check sensor location, heat shielding, and sensor resistance.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal IAT is ambient +5-15°C from underhood heat. Above 60°C the ECU compensation becomes aggressive — retards timing 2-4° which costs 5-10% power.

rules.name: MAF Reading Low Despite High Load
rules.description: MAF reading disproportionately low for the engine load — sensor under-reading.
rules.recommendation: Low MAF with high load means MAF sensor is under-reading airflow. ECU uses MAF for fuel calculation — under-reading = lean. Clean MAF with MAF-specific cleaner (not brake/carb cleaner). Replace if cleaning doesn't help.
rules.technicalNotes: Contaminated hot-wire MAF under-reads by 10-30%. This causes proportional lean fueling. A dirty MAF is one of the most common causes of P0171.

rules.name: MAF Over-Reading at Idle
rules.description: MAF reading higher than expected at idle — sensor over-reading or air leak after MAF.
rules.recommendation: Idle MAF for 1.0-2.0L engines should be 2-5 g/s. Over-reading at idle may be caused by: air leak AFTER the MAF sensor (unmetered air), MAF element contamination reading high, or wrong MAF sensor installed.
rules.technicalNotes: Air leak AFTER MAF: unmetered air enters engine, MAF doesn't see it, but load goes up. Air leak BEFORE MAF: MAF reads ALL air including leak, causing rich fueling.

rules.name: High MAP at Idle (Vacuum Leak)
rules.description: MAP pressure too high at idle — weak engine vacuum indicates significant air leak.
rules.recommendation: Normal idle MAP is 25-45 kPa. Above 60 kPa means very weak vacuum — large air leak bypassing the throttle. Smoke test intake system, check brake booster hose, PCV valve, and intake manifold gasket.
rules.technicalNotes: Each 10 kPa above normal represents a proportionally larger leak. At 70+ kPa, the leak is massive — likely a disconnected hose or cracked intake manifold.

rules.name: MAP Sensor Stuck Reading
rules.description: MAP reading not changing with throttle or load — sensor stuck or hose disconnected.
rules.recommendation: MAP reading near barometric pressure while engine running means: disconnected MAP vacuum hose, cracked hose, or MAP sensor stuck at atmospheric. Reconnect/replace hose. ECU can't calculate load without MAP — causes poor running.
rules.technicalNotes: A disconnected MAP hose reads atmospheric because the sensor port is exposed to ambient pressure instead of intake manifold vacuum.

rules.name: Throttle Body Carbon Buildup
rules.description: High idle RPM with low throttle position — throttle plate not fully closing from carbon deposits.
rules.recommendation: High idle with closed throttle suggests carbon buildup on throttle bore preventing full plate closure, or idle air control valve (IACV) stuck open. Clean throttle body and IACV with throttle cleaner.
rules.technicalNotes: Carbon from PCV blow-by accumulates on throttle plate edge. Even 0.5mm of buildup changes idle airflow significantly.

rules.name: Idle RPM Too Low
rules.description: Idle speed below normal operating range — risk of stalling.
rules.recommendation: Low warm idle (< 600 RPM) causes rough running and risk of stalling. Clean throttle body and IACV, check for vacuum leaks affecting idle control, check IACV wiring and operation.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal warm idle: 650-800 RPM for most 4-cylinder. Below 600 RPM the engine may stall when A/C compressor engages or steering pump loads up.

rules.name: Idle RPM Too High
rules.description: Idle speed significantly above normal — excessive air bypass or throttle issue.
rules.recommendation: High warm idle wastes fuel and indicates IACV stuck open, vacuum leak, throttle cable catching, or drive-by-wire throttle position learning lost. Reset idle learn procedure after cleaning throttle body.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal warm idle: 650-800 RPM. Above 1000 RPM indicates significant unmetered air. A/C cold idle up to 900 RPM is normal.

rules.name: Idle Hunting / Surging
rules.description: Idle RPM oscillating up and down cyclically — ECU hunting for stable idle point.

conditions.description: RPM in idle range but unstable

rules.recommendation: Idle hunting (RPM cycling 200-400 RPM up and down) usually caused by: small vacuum leak confusing idle control, dirty IACV responding erratically, or failing coolant temp sensor causing ECU to oscillate enrichment.
rules.technicalNotes: Hunting differs from rough idle — hunting is rhythmic oscillation (500-900-500-900), while rough idle is irregular variation. TSM idleRpmStdDev captures this.

rules.name: TPS Not Returning to Zero
rules.description: Throttle position not returning to 0% at idle — pedal binding or TPS drift.
rules.recommendation: TPS should read 0-2% at idle. Reading above 8% at rest means: throttle cable too tight, pedal return spring weak, TPS sensor needs recalibration, or carbon buildup preventing plate closure.
rules.technicalNotes: ECU uses TPS for idle recognition. If TPS never reaches zero, idle control strategies may not engage properly.

rules.name: TPS/MAF Disagreement
rules.description: Throttle open but MAF not responding proportionally — air path obstruction or MAF failure.
rules.recommendation: Throttle open but airflow not increasing — severely clogged air filter, MAF sensor failed, air intake duct collapsed/disconnected, or throttle body binding preventing actual opening.
rules.technicalNotes: At 40% TPS, a 1.3-1.5L engine should see 15-25 g/s MAF. Below 10 g/s means major air restriction or MAF sensor failure.

rules.name: O2 Upstream Sensor Dead
rules.description: Upstream O2 voltage fixed at 0V or not responding — sensor heater or element failure.
rules.recommendation: O2 sensor producing no voltage when warm means sensor element has failed or heater circuit is dead. Replace upstream O2 sensor. Check heater fuse first.
rules.technicalNotes: Zirconia O2 element should produce 0.1-0.9V when hot. Fixed at 0V = no output. ECU will default to open-loop operation.

rules.name: O2 Downstream Sensor Dead
rules.description: Downstream O2 voltage fixed near 0V — sensor failure affecting catalyst monitoring.
rules.recommendation: Downstream O2 sensor not producing voltage. This sensor monitors catalyst efficiency — failure means ECU cannot verify catalyst is working. Replace downstream O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Downstream O2 doesn't affect fuel control (that's upstream). But needed for emissions compliance monitoring.

rules.name: O2 Downstream Mimicking Upstream
rules.description: Downstream O2 voltage oscillating like upstream — catalyst not converting properly.
rules.recommendation: Healthy catalyst absorbs O2 fluctuations — downstream should be steady ~0.5-0.7V. If downstream oscillates like upstream, catalyst storage capacity is depleted. Monitor catalyst efficiency ratio.
rules.technicalNotes: Catalyst efficiency = downstream variance / upstream variance. Ratio > 0.5 = degraded, > 0.7 = failed. TSM catalystEfficiencyRatio computes this automatically.

rules.name: O2 Bank 2 Sensor 1 Stuck Lean
rules.description: Bank 2 upstream O2 stuck lean — bank-specific lean condition or sensor failure.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 upstream O2 reading persistent lean. Check for exhaust leak before sensor (false lean), B2-specific vacuum leak, clogged injector on B2 cylinder, or failed B2S1 O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Bank-specific lean with B1 normal strongly suggests bank-specific mechanical fault — not a global fuel delivery issue.

rules.name: O2 Bank 2 Sensor 1 Stuck Rich
rules.description: Bank 2 upstream O2 stuck rich — B2-specific fuel delivery or sensor issue.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 upstream rich. Leaking injector on B2 cylinders, B2S1 O2 sensor contaminated/failed, or incorrect sensor installed (wrong part number for B2).
rules.technicalNotes: B2 rich + B1 normal = bank-specific issue. Both banks rich = global over-fueling.

rules.name: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire detected specifically on cylinder 1 — electrical or mechanical failure isolated to CYL1.
rules.recommendation: Cylinder 1 misfiring. Swap spark plug and coil with another cylinder to isolate: if misfire follows the plug = bad plug. Follows the coil = bad coil. Stays on CYL1 = injector or mechanical (compression).
rules.technicalNotes: Swap test is the most efficient diagnostic: swap suspect component with known-good from another cylinder. If misfire moves, you found the cause.

rules.name: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire detected specifically on cylinder 2.
rules.recommendation: Cylinder 2 misfiring. Perform swap test: move CYL2 plug and coil to another cylinder. If misfire follows — component found. If stays on CYL2 — check injector, compression, valve sealing.
rules.technicalNotes: Same swap test methodology. Document which components were swapped to isolate root cause efficiently.

rules.name: Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire detected specifically on cylinder 3.
rules.recommendation: Cylinder 3 misfiring. Swap test: move CYL3 spark plug and ignition coil to another cylinder. If misfire moves = component. If stays = injector/mechanical.
rules.technicalNotes: On inline-4, CYL3 often shares ignition coil pack with CYL2 in waste-spark system. Check coil pack if both CYL2+3 misfire.

rules.name: Cylinder 4 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire detected specifically on cylinder 4.
rules.recommendation: Cylinder 4 misfiring. Perform component swap test. CYL4 is often the hardest to access — check plug well for oil (leaking valve cover gasket fouling plug).
rules.technicalNotes: Oil in spark plug well (from valve cover gasket) causes tracking and misfire. Common on high-mileage 4-cylinders.

rules.name: Multiple Cylinder Misfire
rules.description: Multiple cylinders misfiring simultaneously — systemic issue rather than single component.
rules.recommendation: Multiple cylinders misfiring = systemic issue: low fuel pressure (all cylinders starving), bad fuel (contaminated), ignition module failure, crank sensor intermittent, cam timing off, or low compression across cylinders.
rules.technicalNotes: P0300 (random/multiple misfire) with individual cylinder codes points to systemic cause. Single cylinder misfire = local cause.

rules.name: Misfire Under Load Only
rules.description: Misfire occurring primarily under load/acceleration — compression or ignition breakdown.
rules.recommendation: Load-dependent misfire: spark plug gap too wide, weak ignition coil breaking down under compression, or low compression on one cylinder. Higher cylinder pressure under load challenges weak ignition.
rules.technicalNotes: Under load, cylinder pressure rises 2-3x. A spark that fires at idle may not fire at 12:1 compression ratio under load. Gap at 0.044 vs spec 0.040 can cause load-only misfire.

rules.name: Timing Retard at High RPM
rules.description: Excessive timing retard at high RPM — knock sensor activity or detonation protection active.
rules.recommendation: At 3500+ RPM, timing should typically be 15-30°. Below 8° means heavy knock retard. Use premium fuel, check for carbon deposits, verify cooling system (hot combustion chambers promote knock).
rules.technicalNotes: Timing below 8° at 3500 RPM causes significant power loss (15-25%). Long-term knock can damage rod bearings.

rules.name: Timing Advance Negative
rules.description: Timing is after TDC (negative advance) — extreme knock retard or sensor error.
rules.recommendation: NEGATIVE timing advance means spark fires AFTER piston passes TDC — this is extreme knock protection or sensor error. Engine will have almost no power. Check fuel quality, knock sensor, cam/crank sensor alignment, and timing chain stretch.
rules.technicalNotes: Negative timing on a computer-controlled engine is almost always a protective measure against severe knock. Root cause must be found — usually fuel, carbon, or timing chain stretch.

rules.name: Timing Over-Advanced at Idle
rules.description: Ignition timing excessively advanced at idle — risk of detonation or rough idle.
rules.recommendation: Idle timing > 25° is unusual. May indicate ECU trying to compensate for low compression (advancing timing to extract power), or cam position sensor drift. Not immediately dangerous but indicates underlying issue.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal idle timing: 8-15° BTDC. Over 25° at idle may indicate the engine needs more advance to run smoothly — usually a compression or valvetrain issue.

rules.name: EGR Stuck Open at Idle
rules.description: EGR valve allowing exhaust gas recirculation at idle — causes rough running and stalling.
rules.recommendation: EGR should be 0% at idle. Any flow at idle dilutes the charge excessively, causing: rough idle, low power, increased MAP, lean fuel trims. Clean or replace EGR valve, check vacuum solenoid.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR carbon buildup is the #1 cause of stuck-open valves. Exhaust soot accumulates on the valve pintle and bore.

rules.name: EGR Position Error Excessive
rules.description: Difference between commanded and actual EGR position is too large — valve not responding.
rules.recommendation: Commanded vs actual EGR position difference > 10% means valve is sticking, binding, or solenoid is weak. Carbon buildup is the most common cause. Remove and clean EGR valve.
rules.technicalNotes: Modern electronic EGR valves have position feedback. Error > 10% will set a DTC. Error 5-10% may not set a code but affects drivability.

rules.name: EGR Flow Insufficient
rules.description: EGR commanded open but MAP not responding — EGR passages blocked or valve not opening.
rules.recommendation: EGR commanded at 20%+ should cause MAP to rise slightly (exhaust pressure entering manifold). If MAP doesn't respond, EGR passages may be carbon-clogged or valve isn't actually opening despite commanded position.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR passage blockage is gradual — starts with reduced NOx control and light DTC, progresses to complete blockage.

rules.name: EVAP Purge Valve Stuck Open
rules.description: EVAP canister purge valve stuck open — fuel vapors flooding engine at idle.
rules.recommendation: EVAP purge valve stuck open floods engine with fuel vapors at idle, causing rich mixture. The ECU compensates with negative LTFT. Replace EVAP purge solenoid valve.
rules.technicalNotes: Purge should be 0% at idle and gradually increase with load/RPM. Stuck-open purge is equivalent to a rich fuel leak — trims go negative to compensate.

rules.name: EVAP No Purge Activity
rules.description: EVAP purge valve never activating — canister not being purged, fuel vapors accumulating.
rules.recommendation: EVAP purge should activate once engine is warm and under load. No activity may mean: purge solenoid stuck closed, wiring fault, ECU not commanding purge (fault mode), or canister vent blocked.
rules.technicalNotes: EVAP purge is emissions-mandatory. Failed purge means fuel vapors vent to atmosphere instead of being burned. Also causes P0441/P0446 eventually.

rules.name: Catalyst Temperature Too High
rules.description: Catalytic converter temperature excessively elevated — risk of substrate meltdown.
rules.recommendation: DANGER: Catalyst over 850°C risks substrate meltdown. Usually caused by misfires dumping unburned fuel into exhaust, or extremely rich running. Fix the cause (misfires) immediately — a melted catalyst is a $1000+ repair.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal cat operating temp: 300-600°C. Above 800°C substrate begins to sinter. Above 1000°C complete meltdown occurs, blocking exhaust.

rules.name: Catalyst Not Reaching Temperature
rules.description: Catalyst temperature not reaching operating range — reduced conversion efficiency.
rules.recommendation: Catalyst light-off temp is ~250°C, normal operating 350-600°C. Below 300°C when engine is warm may indicate: exhaust leak before cat, injector misfire (cool exhaust gas), or sensor fault.
rules.technicalNotes: Below light-off temperature (~250°C) the catalyst cannot convert pollutants. Short trips that never reach this temp accelerate catalyst aging.

rules.name: Alternator Not Compensating for Load
rules.description: Voltage drops when engine load increases — weak alternator or loose belt.
rules.recommendation: At 1500+ RPM with voltage below 13V, alternator isn't keeping up. Belt may be slipping under load, alternator windings may be partially shorted, or voltage regulator is failing. Test alternator output current vs rating.
rules.technicalNotes: Modern alternators should maintain 13.5-14.5V regardless of electrical load. Voltage dropping below 13V at speed means charging capacity is exceeded.

rules.name: Battery Not Charging at Idle
rules.description: Voltage below battery rest level at idle — alternator output insufficient at low RPM.
rules.recommendation: Voltage below 12.4V at idle means battery is depleting even with engine running. Alternator may have lost a phase (partial failure), belt is loose, or battery internal resistance is too high. Test battery health and alternator output.
rules.technicalNotes: A healthy battery at rest reads 12.6V. If running voltage is below that, the battery is being drained while driving — it will eventually die.

rules.name: Overcharging (Battery Damage Risk)
rules.description: System voltage excessively high — voltage regulator failure causing battery overcharge.
rules.recommendation: DANGER: Voltage above 15.5V causes battery overcharging, electrolyte boiling, gas emission (explosion risk), and damage to ECU and electronic components. Stop driving and repair immediately. Replace voltage regulator or alternator.
rules.technicalNotes: Above 15V, battery water decomposes to hydrogen + oxygen (explosive). Above 16V, ECU and sensor damage is possible. Modern batteries have no serviceable water level — overcharging destroys them.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Low at Warm Idle
rules.description: Oil pressure critically low when engine warm and at idle — bearing wear or pump failure.
rules.recommendation: DANGER: Low oil pressure at warm idle means oil film is insufficient to protect bearings. Stop driving immediately. Check oil level, verify with mechanical gauge. Low level = add oil. Full level + low pressure = pump, pickup screen, or bearing clearance worn.
rules.technicalNotes: Minimum safe idle pressure: 70-100 kPa (10-15 psi). Below this, bearing metal-to-metal contact begins. Continued operation will destroy engine.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Not Rising with RPM
rules.description: Oil pressure stays low even as RPM increases — pump output insufficient or major leak.
rules.recommendation: At 2500+ RPM, oil pressure should be 200-400 kPa (30-60 psi). Below 150 kPa means: oil pump worn, relief valve stuck open, severe bearing clearance, or oil level critically low. Do NOT rev the engine further — stop and check oil level.
rules.technicalNotes: Oil pressure = pump output - (bearing clearance leak-back). Worn bearings leak more, reducing pressure at all RPM. This is end-stage engine wear.

rules.name: Oil Temperature Excessive
rules.description: Oil temperature above safe operating range — accelerated degradation.
rules.recommendation: Oil above 130°C is degrading rapidly — viscosity drops, additives break down. Reduce load, check cooling system, verify oil level and condition. Normal oil temp: 90-110°C.
rules.technicalNotes: Every 10°C above 100°C halves oil life. At 130°C, oil life is 1/8 of rated. Sustained 140°+ can cause oil coking in turbo bearings.

rules.name: Oil Fuel Dilution Suspected
rules.description: Persistently rich condition combined with high oil temperature — fuel washing into crankcase.
rules.recommendation: Persistent rich condition causes unburned fuel to wash past piston rings into crankcase, diluting oil. Check oil dipstick for fuel smell, oil level rising (dilution increases volume), and oil viscosity. Change oil and fix rich condition.
rules.technicalNotes: Fuel dilution reduces oil viscosity and load-carrying capacity. More than 3% fuel in oil requires immediate oil change.

rules.name: Transmission Overheating While Driving
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature dangerously elevated during normal driving.
rules.recommendation: Transmission fluid above 120°C causes rapid fluid degradation, seal hardening, and clutch pack damage. Stop driving, let cool. Check fluid level and condition (burnt smell = damage already occurring). Towing in this condition shortens trans life dramatically.
rules.technicalNotes: ATF life halves every 10°C above 90°C. At 120°C, ATF life is 1/8. At 130°C, clutch pack friction material begins to glaze.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Not Warming Up
rules.description: Transmission fluid still cold after extended driving — cooler bypass stuck or thermostat issue.
rules.recommendation: Transmission fluid should reach 60-80°C within 10 minutes of driving. Persistently cold fluid increases viscosity, slows shifts, and increases fuel consumption. Check trans cooler thermostat (if equipped) and cooler line routing.
rules.technicalNotes: Cold ATF viscosity is 10-100x higher than operating temp. This causes harsh shifts and increased pump effort.

rules.name: Boost Leak Detected
rules.description: Boost pressure not building despite turbo RPM and load — intercooler or charge pipe leak.
rules.recommendation: At load and RPM, boost pressure should exceed atmospheric. No boost means: charge pipe leak, intercooler leak, turbo wastegate stuck open, or turbo not spinning. Check all intercooler connections and boost system for leaks.
rules.technicalNotes: Boost leak testing with smoke or pressure test is definitive. Even a small leak (2mm hole) can prevent full boost.

rules.name: Overboost Condition
rules.description: Boost pressure exceeding safe limits — wastegate not limiting turbo output.
rules.recommendation: Overboost is dangerous — excessive cylinder pressure can blow head gasket, crack pistons, or damage rods. Wastegate actuator stuck closed, wastegate line disconnected, or boost controller malfunction. Stop hard acceleration immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: Most factory turbos are calibrated for 0.3-0.7 bar boost. Exceeding by even 0.2 bar raises cylinder pressure significantly. Intercooler pressure limits apply.

rules.name: Sustained High Load Operation
rules.description: Engine operating at high load for extended period — increased stress on all systems.
rules.recommendation: VExtended high-load operation (hill climb, towing, spirited driving) accelerates oil degradation, increases coolant temp, and stresses fuel system. Monitor temperatures. Consider shorter oil change interval if this is typical driving pattern.
rules.technicalNotes: High load operation is within design parameters but accelerates wear on all fluids and components. Important context for oil life and cooling assessment.

rules.name: Low Barometric Pressure (High Altitude)
rules.description: Low barometric pressure detected — engine naturally produces less power at altitude.
rules.recommendation: Low barometric pressure means less air density — engine produces 3% less power per 300m elevation. This is normal, not a fault. Fuel trims may shift slightly lean as ECU compensates for thinner air.
rules.technicalNotes: Malaysian context: highland destinations (Cameron Highlands ~1500m, Genting ~1700m) will show baro drop of 15-20 kPa, causing 5-6% power loss. This is physics, not a fault.

rules.name: MAF/MAP Sensor Disagreement
rules.description: MAF and MAP readings don't correlate — one sensor is likely reading incorrectly.
rules.recommendation: High MAF with very low MAP is contradictory — high airflow should mean higher MAP (lower vacuum). One sensor is reading wrong. Compare to engine load to determine which is plausible.
rules.technicalNotes: MAF and MAP are correlated — more air = higher MAP. If they disagree, the ECU may use the wrong one for fueling, causing either rich or lean condition.

rules.name: TPS/Relative TPS Mismatch
rules.description: Primary and relative throttle position sensors disagree — pedal position tracking error.
rules.recommendation: On drive-by-wire vehicles, dual TPS signals must agree. Disagreement > 10% may trigger limp mode. Check throttle body wiring, connectors, and TPS sensor. May need TPS learn/reset procedure after replacement.
rules.technicalNotes: Dual TPS is a safety feature — if sensors disagree, ECU limits throttle opening to protect against unintended acceleration.

rules.name: Speed Sensor Implausible
rules.description: Vehicle speed reading doesn't match RPM and gear — sensor fault or wiring issue.
rules.recommendation: Engine loaded but speed reads zero — vehicle speed sensor (VSS) may be faulty. This affects: cruise control, speedometer, transmission shift points, ABS, and idle speed. Check VSS connector, tone ring, and wiring.
rules.technicalNotes: Faulty VSS causes harsh/delayed shifts (trans doesn't know speed), disabled cruise control, and can cause idle flare when stopping.

rules.name: High Load at Low Throttle
rules.description: Engine load disproportionately high for throttle opening — possible restriction or leak.
rules.recommendation: High load despite low throttle can indicate: vacuum leak (unmetered air raising load), EGR stuck open (exhaust gas inflating load), or climbing a hill in too high a gear. Also check A/C compressor engagement.
rules.technicalNotes: Calculated load = actual airflow / theoretical maximum. Unmetered air (vacuum leak) raises actual flow without throttle opening — load appears high.

rules.name: Low Load Despite High Throttle
rules.description: Engine load barely rising despite wide throttle — air intake severely restricted.
rules.recommendation: Throttle open but load doesn't increase — severely clogged air filter, collapsed intake hose, or throttle body not actually opening (drive-by-wire fault). Check air filter first (cheapest, most common).
rules.technicalNotes: At 50% TPS, load should be at least 40-60% on a healthy engine. Below 30% means major airflow restriction.

rules.name: Excessive Warm-Up Enrichment
rules.description: Very rich fuel trims during cold start — ECU adding excessive cold-start fuel.
rules.recommendation: Some cold start enrichment is normal, but LTFT < -15% during warmup suggests ECU's cold enrichment map is too aggressive for current conditions, or injectors are leaking/adding extra fuel. May cause plug fouling.
rules.technicalNotes: Malaysian cold starts rarely see ECT below 25°C. If ECT sensor reads lower, enrichment will be excessive for actual conditions.

rules.name: Lean Condition with Misfire
rules.description: Lean fuel trims AND misfires occurring — insufficient fuel causing incomplete combustion.
rules.recommendation: Lean misfire is dangerous — unburned fuel passes to exhaust, overheating catalyst. The combination confirms fuel delivery failure (not just sensor error). Immediately check: fuel pump pressure, fuel filter, vacuum hoses.
rules.technicalNotes: Lean misfire → unburned O2 reaches catalyst → exothermic reaction with any HC → catalyst overheat. This is how misfires destroy catalysts.

rules.name: Rich Running with Catalyst Stress
rules.description: Rich trims combined with elevated catalyst temperature — catalyst converting excess fuel to heat.
rules.recommendation: Rich exhaust forces the catalyst to oxidize excess fuel, generating heat. Sustained rich running will overheat and damage catalyst. Fix the rich condition (leaking injector, fuel pressure, EVAP purge stuck) before catalyst damage occurs.
rules.technicalNotes: The catalyst oxidizes HC and CO — excess fuel means more exothermic reaction. Normal cat temp = 350-600°C. Above 650°C from rich running indicates significant fuel overload.

rules.name: Open Loop While Driving
rules.description: ECU in open-loop fuel control while vehicle is being driven — emissions and efficiency degraded.
rules.recommendation: ECU should be in closed loop during normal driving. Open loop at cruise means ECU has disabled O2 feedback — either a sensor fault or ECU fault mode. Fuel efficiency drops 10-15% and emissions increase significantly.
rules.technicalNotes: Open loop during WOT enrichment and cold start is normal. Open loop during steady cruising is always a fault condition.

rules.name: MAF Idle Airflow Implausible
rules.description: MAF reading at idle doesn't match expected value for engine displacement.
rules.recommendation: For 1.0-2.0L engines, idle MAF should be 2-5 g/s. Above 7 g/s at idle means either: unmetered air entering after MAF, MAF sensor over-reading, or engine is actually not at proper idle (higher load from A/C, power steering, etc.).
rules.technicalNotes: Rule of thumb: 1 g/s per 100cc displacement per 1000 RPM. A 1500cc at 750 RPM = 11.25 × 0.7 VE ≈ 8 g/s theoretical, but idle VE is ~40% so expect 3-5 g/s.

rules.name: Fuel System in Fault Mode
rules.description: Fuel system status indicates open-loop due to detected fault — degraded operation.
rules.recommendation: Fuel system status byte value 8 means ECU has detected a fault and is running in open-loop fault mode. This is a fail-safe — fuel control is degraded. Scan DTCs to find the triggering fault.
rules.technicalNotes: Status 8 = open-loop-fault. Status 1 = open-loop warmup. Status 2 = closed-loop. Status 4 = open-loop drive condition (WOT). Only 2 is fully healthy during normal cruising.

rules.name: Short Trip Driving Pattern
rules.description: Engine frequently not reaching full operating temperature — accelerates wear and contamination.
rules.recommendation: This scan shows a short trip pattern — engine didn't reach full operating temperature. Repeated short trips cause: moisture contamination of oil, fuel dilution, incomplete EVAP purge, and accelerated engine wear. Consider combining trips or occasional longer drives.
rules.technicalNotes: Below 80°C, crankcase moisture doesn't evaporate. Water in oil forms sludge and acids. OEMs recommend oil changes twice as often for short-trip-only drivers.

rules.name: Extended Idle Operation
rules.description: Engine idling for extended period — increased carbon buildup and oil stress.
rules.recommendation: Extended idling: low oil pressure at idle accelerates bearing wear, incomplete combustion causes carbon on intake valves, and coolant circulation is minimal. If frequently idling > 10 min, consider more frequent oil changes.
rules.technicalNotes: Many OEMs consider 1 hour idle equivalent to 30 km of driving for oil life calculation. Idle is 'severe condition' in most maintenance schedules.

rules.name: High RPM Operation Detected
rules.description: Engine operating at high RPM — within design limits but increased mechanical stress.
rules.recommendation: High RPM operation increases: valve float risk, oil shearing stress, bearing loads, and heat generation. Occasional high RPM is fine (clears carbon), but sustained high RPM accelerates component wear. Ensure oil is fresh and correct viscosity.
rules.technicalNotes: Most NA 4-cylinders redline at 6000-7000 RPM. Operating above 5500 for extended periods increases valve spring fatigue and piston ring wear.

rules.name: Wrong Gear for Conditions (Lugging)
rules.description: Engine under high load at low RPM — possible gear too high / engine lugging.
rules.recommendation: High load at low RPM (lugging) causes pre-ignition/knock risk, low oil pressure, and excessive drivetrain stress. Downshift to bring RPM above 2000 before applying heavy throttle.
rules.technicalNotes: Lugging keeps combustion chamber temperatures high with insufficient airflow for cooling. This promotes carbon buildup and knock. Modern ECUs retard timing to protect, but at a power and efficiency cost.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Slow Rich-to-Lean Transition
rules.description: Upstream O2 voltage stuck high (rich) — slow transition to lean indicates sensor aging.
rules.recommendation: O2 reading rich but LTFT is lean-compensating — O2 sensor may be biased high (reading richer than actual). This causes ECU to lean out the mixture based on false rich signal. Replace upstream O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: O2 sensor bias is subtle — sensor works but reads offset from actual AFR. Results in chronic lean condition that ECU doesn't detect because O2 'says' rich.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Slow Lean-to-Rich Transition
rules.description: Upstream O2 voltage stuck low (lean) — slow transition to rich indicates sensor aging.
rules.recommendation: O2 reading lean but LTFT is rich-compensating — O2 sensor biased low (reading leaner than actual). ECU enriches based on false lean signal. Replace upstream O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Always cross-reference O2 voltage with fuel trim direction. If they disagree (O2 says lean but trims are negative/rich), the O2 sensor is the liar.

rules.name: Catalyst Monitor Incomplete
rules.description: Downstream O2 sensor not producing enough data to verify catalyst health.
rules.recommendation: Downstream O2 reading flat at ~0.45V while upstream switches is NORMAL for a healthy catalyst. If this reading persists AND no DTC, catalyst is likely working well. This is an informational finding.
rules.technicalNotes: Flat downstream O2 at ~0.45V is actually the IDEAL catalyst reading — it means cat is absorbing all O2 fluctuations.

rules.name: MAP Reading Above Barometric at Idle
rules.description: MAP sensor reading exceeds barometric pressure at idle on NA engine — sensor error.
rules.recommendation:
On a naturally aspirated engine, MAP at idle should be 25-50 kPa (strong vacuum). MAP at 95+ kPa at idle means:
1) MAP sensor hose disconnected (reading atmosphere),
2) MAP sensor itself failed, or
3) Massive vacuum leak.
rules.technicalNotes: Quick test: disconnect MAP hose — if reading doesn't change, hose was already disconnected or sensor is stuck.

rules.name: DPF Soot Loading High
rules.description: DPF differential pressure elevated — soot accumulation needs passive or active regeneration.
rules.recommendation: DPF soot loading is increasing. To enable passive regeneration: drive at sustained 60+ km/h for 15-20 minutes. Avoid frequent short trips that don't allow exhaust temp to reach regen threshold (>550°C).
rules.technicalNotes: Passive regen occurs when exhaust temp naturally reaches 550°C+ during sustained driving. Active regen (ECU-triggered post-injection) activates when soot reaches ~45% capacity.

rules.name: Turbo RPM Low Despite High Load
rules.description: Turbocharger not spooling despite engine demand — bearing failure or exhaust restriction.
rules.recommendation: At 2500+ RPM and 60%+ load, turbo should be fully spooled. Low turbo RPM suggests: turbo bearing failure (shaft drag), exhaust restriction before turbo, VGT actuator stuck, or exhaust leak.
rules.technicalNotes: Check for shaft play (radial and axial). Any noticeable play = turbo replacement. Also listen for metallic whine/scraping = compressor wheel contact.

rules.name: Barometric Pressure Implausible
rules.description: Barometric pressure reading outside physically possible range.
rules.recommendation: Barometric pressure should be 85-105 kPa at any reasonable elevation. Reading outside 80-110 kPa means sensor failure. Baro is used for fuel and timing calculations — bad reading affects performance and emissions.
commonParts: Barometric pressure sensor (or MAP sensor with baro function)
rules.technicalNotes: Many ECUs derive baro from MAP at key-on before cranking. If MAP sensor is faulty, both MAP and baro readings will be wrong.

rules.name: Low Fuel Pressure at Idle
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure below specification at idle — fuel pump or regulator issue.
rules.recommendation: Low fuel pressure at idle may cause lean misfires and rough idle. Check fuel pump output pressure, fuel filter condition, and fuel pressure regulator.
rules.technicalNotes: Port injection systems typically require 250-380 kPa. Direct injection systems run 3000-15000 kPa and have separate high-pressure pump.

rules.name: Excessive Fuel Pressure
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure above specification — stuck regulator or restricted return line.
rules.recommendation: High fuel pressure causes rich running, fouled spark plugs, and potential catalytic converter damage. Inspect fuel pressure regulator diaphragm and return line for kinks or blockage.
rules.technicalNotes: Stuck closed regulator allows pump to build maximum pressure. Check regulator vacuum line for fuel contamination (leaking diaphragm).

rules.name: Rapid STFT Oscillation
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim oscillating rapidly ±15% — O2 sensor feedback loop instability.
rules.recommendation: Rapid fuel trim swings indicate unstable air-fuel ratio control. Check O2 sensor response time, vacuum leaks, and injector flow balance.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal STFT oscillation is ±3-5% at 1-2 Hz. Amplitude > 15% or frequency > 5 Hz indicates control loop instability.

rules.name: Combined Fuel Trim at Lean Limit
rules.description: STFT + LTFT exceeds +25% — ECU at maximum lean compensation.
rules.recommendation: ECU has reached maximum fuel compensation. Engine is critically lean. This causes misfires, overheating, and catalyst damage. Stop driving and diagnose immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: Most ECUs limit STFT to ±25% and LTFT to ±25%. When combined trims exceed 25%, the system cannot maintain stoichiometric ratio.

rules.name: Combined Fuel Trim at Rich Limit
rules.description: STFT + LTFT below -25% — ECU at maximum rich compensation.
rules.recommendation: ECU at maximum rich compensation. Excess fuel washes cylinder walls, dilutes oil, and damages catalyst. Investigate leaking injectors, high fuel pressure, or coolant leak into combustion chamber.
rules.technicalNotes: Rich condition at idle only may indicate leaking injector. Rich at all RPM suggests fuel pressure regulator or FPR vacuum line leak.

rules.name: Bank 2 Lean Independent of Bank 1
rules.description: Bank 2 running lean while Bank 1 trims are normal — bank-specific issue.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 lean with Bank 1 normal isolates the issue to Bank 2 only. Check for intake manifold gasket leak on Bank 2 side, clogged injector on Bank 2, or exhaust leak before Bank 2 O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: On V-engines, bank-specific trim issues are common. Use smoke machine to isolate vacuum leaks on the affected bank.

rules.name: Bank 2 Rich Independent of Bank 1
rules.description: Bank 2 running rich while Bank 1 trims are normal — Bank 2 specific fault.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 running rich independently. Check for leaking injector on Bank 2, O2 B2S1 sensor bias, or restricted exhaust on Bank 2.
rules.technicalNotes: Leaking injector causes rich at idle, normalizes at load. O2 sensor bias affects all conditions equally.

rules.name: Critically Low Fuel Level
rules.description: Fuel tank below 5% — fuel pump overheating risk and potential air ingestion.
rules.recommendation: Running on very low fuel exposes the in-tank fuel pump to air, causing overheating and premature failure. Fuel also acts as pump coolant. Refuel immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: SAE J2452: Electric fuel pumps are cooled by surrounding fuel. Sustained operation below 1/8 tank reduces pump life by 40-60%.

rules.name: Moderate LTFT Positive Drift
rules.description: Long-term fuel trim drifting positive 5-10% — developing lean condition.
rules.recommendation: Mild positive LTFT drift indicates a developing lean condition. Monitor trend. If increasing over time, investigate vacuum leaks, MAF contamination, or fuel delivery degradation.
rules.technicalNotes: LTFT 5-10% is within normal adaptation range for many vehicles but trending higher warrants attention.

rules.name: Moderate LTFT Negative Drift
rules.description: Long term fuel trim drifting negative 5-10% — developing rich condition.
rules.recommendation: Mild negative LTFT drift indicates developing rich condition. Could be early sign of leaking injector, high fuel pressure, or charcoal canister purge issue. Monitor trend.
rules.technicalNotes: Negative LTFT with fuel smell may indicate injector leak. Check oil level for fuel dilution.

rules.name: Severe Engine Overheating
rules.description: Coolant temperature exceeds 120°C — immediate engine damage risk.
rules.recommendation: STOP DRIVING IMMEDIATELY. Pull over safely and turn off engine. Coolant above 120°C causes head gasket failure, warped cylinder head, and seized engine. Call for tow.
rules.technicalNotes: Aluminum heads warp above 120°C. Cast iron blocks can tolerate slightly more but risk is severe. Do NOT open radiator cap when hot.

rules.name: ECT Sensor Reading Implausibly High
rules.description: Coolant temperature reading exceeds 140°C — likely sensor failure rather than actual overheating.
rules.recommendation: ECT reading above 140°C is more likely a sensor fault than actual temperature. Check ECT sensor connector for corrosion, wiring for damage, and sensor resistance against specification.
rules.technicalNotes: NTC thermistor ECT sensors: ~2.5kΩ at 20°C, ~300Ω at 80°C, ~85Ω at 120°C. Shorted to ground reads as very high temp.

rules.name: ECT Sensor Reading Implausibly Low
rules.description: Coolant temperature reading stays below 30°C after extended running — sensor open circuit.
rules.recommendation: Coolant should reach at least 50°C after 10 minutes of running. Reading below 30°C indicates ECT sensor open circuit or disconnected. ECU will use default temp, causing rich running and poor fuel economy.
rules.technicalNotes: Open circuit ECT reads maximum resistance = minimum temperature. ECU defaults to 80°C for fueling, causing rich warmup.

rules.name: Thermostat Partially Stuck Open
rules.description: Coolant temperature stabilizes below 80°C at highway speed — thermostat not fully closing.
rules.recommendation: Thermostat should close to maintain 87-95°C. Partially stuck open thermostat reduces fuel economy 5-10% and increases engine wear. Replace thermostat.
rules.technicalNotes: Wax pellet thermostats fail open, which is the safe failure mode. Operating below 80°C prevents closed-loop fuel control on some ECUs.

rules.name: Cooling Fan Running Continuously
rules.description: Engine cool but fan behavior suggests it's always on — stuck relay or ECT signal fault.
rules.recommendation: Cooling fan should only activate above 95-100°C. Constant fan operation drains battery, adds parasitic load, and prevents engine from reaching optimal operating temperature quickly.
rules.technicalNotes: Electric cooling fans draw 15-30A. Stuck relay or shorted ECT sensor ground can cause continuous fan operation.

rules.name: Extremely High Intake Air Temperature
rules.description: Intake air temperature above 70°C — severe heat soak or intake routing issue.
rules.recommendation: Intake air above 70°C significantly reduces engine power and increases knock risk. Check air filter box for hot air ingestion, missing cold air duct, or heat shield damage near intake. Each 10°C rise in IAT reduces power ~3%.
rules.technicalNotes: Ideal IAT is ambient + 5-15°C. Heat soak after shutdown can cause IAT 80-100°C on first restart. Should normalize within 2 min of driving.

rules.name: IAT Sensor Implausibly Cold
rules.description: Intake air temp reading below -20°C — sensor open circuit or disconnected.
rules.recommendation: IAT below -20°C is implausible except in extreme arctic conditions. Likely IAT sensor open circuit. ECU will default to a fixed IAT value, affecting fuel calculation accuracy.
rules.technicalNotes: IAT sensor (NTC): ~10kΩ at 0°C, ~2.5kΩ at 25°C. Open circuit reads maximum resistance = very cold temperature.

rules.name: MAF Reading Too High at Idle
rules.description: MAF sensor reporting excessive airflow at idle — sensor contamination or post-MAF leak.
rules.recommendation: Typical idle MAF for 1.3-2.0L engine is 2-5 g/s. Reading above 8 g/s at idle suggests MAF sensor over-reading, post-MAF air leak, or stuck-open idle air control valve.
rules.technicalNotes: MAF formula: idle MAF ≈ (RPM × displacement × VE × 1.184) / 3456. For 1.5L at 750 RPM with 85% VE ≈ 3.9 g/s.

rules.name: MAF Reading Zero While Engine Running
rules.description: MAF sensor reporting 0 g/s airflow while engine is running — complete sensor failure.
rules.recommendation: MAF reading zero while engine runs means complete sensor failure. ECU will use backup speed-density mode (MAP + IAT) but with reduced performance. Replace MAF sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Many ECUs have limp-home mode using MAP+IAT when MAF fails. Performance significantly degraded in backup mode.

rules.name: High MAP at Highway Cruise
rules.description: Manifold pressure too high at cruise — indicates restriction in exhaust or cam timing issue.
rules.recommendation: At highway cruise with part throttle, MAP should be 35-55 kPa for NA engines. High MAP indicates reduced engine vacuum — possible clogged catalyst, late cam timing, or valve sealing issue.
rules.technicalNotes: Exhaust restriction test: at 2500 RPM, manifold vacuum should be 15-20 inHg (50-68 kPa vacuum). Lower vacuum = higher MAP.
