rules.name: TPS Not Returning to Zero
rules.description: Throttle position sensor not reading near 0% when expected closed — carbon buildup or sensor offset.
rules.recommendation: TPS should read 0-3% at closed throttle idle. Reading above 5% indicates carbon buildup preventing full closure, TPS sensor offset, or throttle body sticking.
rules.technicalNotes: Clean throttle body with approved cleaner. Some ECUs require TPS relearn procedure after cleaning.

rules.name: Idle RPM Too High
rules.description: Engine idle speed above 1100 RPM when warm — vacuum leak or idle control issue.
rules.recommendation: Warm idle should be 650-800 RPM for most engines. High idle when warm suggests vacuum leak, stuck IAC valve, or throttle body not closing fully.
rules.technicalNotes: Check for unmetered air entering intake: brake booster hose, PCV valve, EVAP purge valve stuck open.

rules.name: Idle RPM Dangerously Low
rules.description: Idle speed below 550 RPM — near stall condition.
rules.recommendation: Idle below 550 RPM risks stalling, especially with AC or electrical load. Check for dirty throttle body, failing IAC motor, or restricted idle air passage.
rules.technicalNotes: AC compressor engagement adds ~50 RPM load. Power steering at lock adds ~100 RPM load. ECU should compensate for these loads.

rules.name: Idle Hunting — RPM Oscillation
rules.description: RPM oscillating 200+ RPM at idle — idle air control instability.
rules.recommendation: Idle hunting where RPM repeatedly rises and falls indicates unstable idle air control. Check for intermittent vacuum leak, dirty IAC valve, or ECU hunting for correct idle setpoint.
rules.technicalNotes: Classic hunting pattern: 200-300 RPM oscillation at 0.5-1 Hz. Often caused by cracked vacuum hose that opens/closes with vacuum cycling.

rules.name: Relative TPS Mismatch with Main TPS
rules.description: Relative throttle position disagrees with commanded TPS — possible sensor deviation.
rules.recommendation: Two TPS readings should track proportionally. Significant mismatch indicates one sensor drifting. Most ECUs use the lower reading for safety, reducing available power.
rules.technicalNotes: Modern throttle bodies have redundant TPS (TPS1 + TPS2) with inverse slopes for validation. P2135 = correlation fault between the two.

rules.name: O2 Upstream Stuck at Mid-Voltage
rules.description: Upstream O2 sensor stuck near 0.45V — sensor not switching, likely failed heater or sensor.
rules.recommendation: Upstream O2 stuck at bias voltage (0.45V) indicates sensor not responding to exhaust changes. Likely failed O2 heater or sensor element. ECU will use fixed fuel map, reducing efficiency.
rules.technicalNotes: Zirconia O2 sensors have ~0.45V bias when cold or dead. After heater reaches 350°C (30-60s), voltage should swing 0.1-0.9V.

rules.name: O2 Upstream Stuck Lean
rules.description: Upstream O2 persistently below 0.2V — sensor stuck lean or actual severe lean condition.
rules.recommendation: O2 stuck lean with high positive LTFT indicates either genuine severe lean condition or O2 sensor reporting falsely lean. Propane test at exhaust can differentiate: if O2 stays lean with propane, sensor is faulty.
rules.technicalNotes: False lean O2 can be caused by exhaust leak before sensor, allowing ambient air to dilute exhaust gas at the sensor tip.

rules.name: O2 Upstream Stuck Rich
rules.description: Upstream O2 persistently above 0.8V — sensor stuck rich or actual rich condition.
rules.recommendation: O2 stuck rich with negative LTFT. If truly rich, check for leaking injector, high fuel pressure, or coolant entering combustion. If sensor false-reading, silicone contamination or aged sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Silicone contamination from RTV sealant fumes or fuel additives causes O2 sensor to read falsely rich. White deposit on sensor tip is diagnostic.

rules.name: Downstream O2 Actively Switching
rules.description: Post-cat O2 sensor switching like upstream sensor — catalyst has failed.
rules.recommendation: Post-catalyst O2 should remain stable at 0.5-0.7V. Active switching indicates catalyst is no longer storing/releasing oxygen — catalyst efficiency has fallen below threshold. Replacement required for emissions compliance.
rules.technicalNotes: Catalyst efficiency ratio = downstream_variance / upstream_variance. Healthy < 0.3, borderline 0.3-0.5, failed > 0.5.

rules.name: O2 Bank 2 Sensor Lean Bias
rules.description: Bank 2 O2 sensor consistently lower than Bank 1 — sensor aging or bank-specific issue.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 upstream O2 reading consistently lean may indicate sensor aging, exhaust leak on Bank 2, or injector imbalance. Compare Bank 1 and Bank 2 fuel trims for confirmation.
rules.technicalNotes: Replace O2 sensors in pairs on V-engines when one shows aging. Response time should be < 100ms for lean-to-rich transition.

rules.name: Single Cylinder Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire count elevated on one cylinder — coil, plug, or injector for that cylinder.
rules.recommendation: Isolated single cylinder misfire. Swap coil with adjacent cylinder — if misfire follows coil, replace coil. If stays, check spark plug and then injector for that cylinder.
rules.technicalNotes: Coil-on-plug systems: swap test is definitive. Wasted spark systems: each coil fires two cylinders, so companion cylinder should also misfire if coil fails.

rules.name: Multiple Cylinder Misfires
rules.description: Misfires on multiple cylinders simultaneously — system-wide issue, not cylinder-specific.
rules.recommendation: Multiple cylinder misfires indicate system-wide issue: fuel pressure problem, vacuum leak, ignition module failure, or severe timing issue. Not cylinder-specific components.
rules.technicalNotes: P0300 (random misfire) vs cylinder-specific codes. Random/multiple = system cause. Single = component cause.

rules.name: Timing Severely Retarded
rules.description: Ignition timing below 0° — ECU pulling timing aggressively due to knock or sensor issue.
rules.recommendation: Negative timing advance means ECU is actively retarding timing past TDC. This is an emergency knock response that severely reduces power and increases exhaust temperature. Check fuel octane, knock sensor operation, and for carbon deposits.
rules.technicalNotes: Each degree of timing retard reduces torque ~2% and increases exhaust temp ~10°C. Sustained negative timing causes catalyst overheating.

rules.name: Excessive Timing Advance
rules.description: Ignition timing above 40° at moderate RPM — timing chain stretched or sensor error.
rules.recommendation: Most engines are calibrated for maximum 25-35° advance. Reading above 40° may indicate stretched timing chain, incorrect crank sensor installation, or ECU calibration error. Risk of pre-ignition and piston damage.
rules.technicalNotes: Stretched timing chain causes cam-crank correlation error. ECU reads crank position but actual piston position is retarded, so reported advance appears higher.

rules.name: Timing Not Advancing with RPM
rules.description: Timing stuck at idle value regardless of RPM — advance mechanism or knock control fault.
rules.recommendation: Timing should advance from 10-15° at idle to 25-35° at 3000+ RPM. Stuck timing suggests knock sensor input preventing advance, or ECU in failsafe mode.
rules.technicalNotes: Knock sensor fault: ECU defaults to retarded timing map for engine protection. Diagnose knock sensor circuit before replacing.

rules.name: EGR Valve Stuck Closed
rules.description: EGR commanded but zero actual flow detected — valve stuck closed or passage blocked.
rules.recommendation: EGR valve not opening when commanded. Carbon buildup in EGR passage is the most common cause. Remove and clean EGR valve and passage. If valve actuator has failed, replace EGR valve.
rules.technicalNotes: Carbon deposits from exhaust gas block EGR passages over time. Use intake cleaner or walnut shell blast. EGR flow insufficient = P0401.

rules.name: EGR Causing Idle Instability
rules.description: EGR valve leaking at idle — introducing exhaust gas into idle airflow causing surging.

conditions.description: EGR should be 0 at idle but showing flow

rules.recommendation: EGR should be fully closed at idle. Any EGR flow at idle dilutes the combustion charge with inert gas, causing rough idle, stalling, and misfires. Clean or replace EGR valve.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR stuck partially open: carbon on seat prevents full closure. Symptoms worse at idle because small idle air volume is significantly diluted.

rules.name: EVAP Purge Valve Stuck Closed
rules.description: EVAP purge commanded but no flow detected — charcoal canister vapors not being purged.
rules.recommendation: EVAP purge should activate after warmup to remove stored fuel vapors from charcoal canister. Stuck purge valve causes canister to saturate, eventually releasing hydrocarbons. Check purge solenoid and vacuum line.
rules.technicalNotes: Purge typically activates above 80°C ECT, after 2-3 minutes of running. Command starts at 5-10% duty cycle and increases.

rules.name: EVAP System Large Leak Indicator
rules.description: Fuel tank level dropping faster than consumption rate — possible EVAP leak or cap issue.

conditions.description: Low enough fuel to notice loss

rules.recommendation: High EVAP purge command with decreasing fuel level may indicate EVAP system leak. Check fuel cap seal, EVAP hose connections, and charcoal canister for cracks. Fuel smell is a strong indicator.
rules.technicalNotes: EVAP leak detection: ECU uses purge and vent valves to pressurize/vacuum test the system. P0455 = large leak (>0.040"), P0456 = small leak (>0.020").

rules.name: Catalyst Overtemperature
rules.description: Catalyst temperature exceeds 800°C — risk of catalyst meltdown.
rules.recommendation: CRITICAL: Catalyst temperature above 800°C causes substrate meltdown. Immediately reduce load and investigate cause — usually sustained rich running or misfires sending unburned fuel to catalyst.
rules.technicalNotes: Ceramic substrate melts at ~1400°C but thermal stress cracking starts at 800°C. Exothermic burn of unburned HC in catalyst generates enormous heat.

rules.name: Catalyst Below Light-Off Temperature
rules.description: Catalyst temperature below 250°C after extended driving — cat not functioning.
rules.recommendation: Catalyst light-off typically occurs at 250-300°C, reached within 2-3 minutes. If still cold after 10 minutes of driving, check for exhaust leak before catalyst, incorrect sensor location, or engine running too lean (less exhaust heat).
rules.technicalNotes: Light-off = temperature at which catalyst reaches 50% conversion efficiency. Typically 250-300°C for three-way catalysts.

rules.name: Extended Open Loop Driving
rules.description: Fuel system still in open loop after extended driving — O2 sensor or ECT issue preventing closed loop.
rules.recommendation: Closed loop should engage within 60-90 seconds. Extended open loop means O2 sensors not providing valid feedback. Check O2 heater circuits, ECT sensor reading, and for stored O2 sensor codes.
rules.technicalNotes: Closed loop entry conditions: ECT > threshold (usually 60-70°C), O2 sensor active, no enrichment events, runtime > 30s.

rules.name: DPF High Backpressure
rules.description: Diesel particulate filter creating excessive exhaust backpressure — soot overloaded.
rules.recommendation: High DPF differential pressure means soot is accumulating. Perform a forced regeneration drive (sustained highway speed 80+ km/h for 20 min). If pressure doesn't drop, DPF may need professional cleaning or replacement.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal DPF diff pressure: 2-8 kPa. Above 12 kPa triggers regen request. Above 20 kPa = force warning. Short trip driving prevents passive regen.

rules.name: DPF Regeneration Temperature Insufficient
rules.description: DPF temperature too low for effective regeneration — soot not burning off.
rules.recommendation: DPF regeneration requires 550-650°C to burn accumulated soot. Temperature below 400°C means regen is failing. Drive at sustained highway speed. If unable, professional forced regen is needed.
rules.technicalNotes: Passive regen: occurs naturally above 350°C with NO2 catalyst. Active regen: ECU injects extra fuel to raise exhaust temp to 550-650°C.

rules.name: Alternator Overcharging
rules.description: System voltage above 15V — voltage regulator failure risking battery and electronics damage.
rules.recommendation: Voltage above 15V indicates voltage regulator failure. This overcharges the battery, causing electrolyte boiling, plate warping, and damages sensitive electronics. Replace alternator or external regulator immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: Modern MOSFET regulators rarely fail high — more common in older brush-type regulators. Check alternator output pin for short to field winding.

rules.name: Alternator Low Output
rules.description: System voltage below 13.0V while engine running — alternator not charging adequately.
rules.recommendation: With engine running above 1000 RPM, voltage should be 13.5-14.5V. Below 13V means alternator output is insufficient. Check drive belt tension, alternator brushes, and battery terminal connections.
rules.technicalNotes: Belt tension: deflection should be 10-15mm under 10kg force. Worn belt slips at low RPM/high load. Check for glazed belt surface.

rules.name: Battery Sulfation Indicator
rules.description: Voltage drops below 12V at idle — battery not holding charge.
rules.recommendation: Voltage dropping below 12V even at idle suggests the battery cannot support the electrical load. Have battery tested for capacity and internal resistance. Sulfated batteries cannot recover.
rules.technicalNotes: Lead-acid battery: 12.6V = 100% charge, 12.4V = 75%, 12.0V = 25%. Below 12.4V resting indicates degradation. CCA test is more definitive.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Critically Low at Idle
rules.description: Oil pressure below 80 kPa at warm idle — bearing clearance or pump issue.
rules.recommendation: CRITICAL: Oil pressure below 80 kPa at warm idle puts bearing surfaces at risk. Stop engine promptly. Check oil level immediately. If oil level is OK, oil pump, pickup screen, or bearing clearance issue requires professional diagnosis.
rules.technicalNotes: Minimum safe idle oil pressure: 10 psi (69 kPa). Below this, hydrodynamic lubrication film breaks down and metal-to-metal contact occurs.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Drops Under Load
rules.description: Oil pressure paradoxically decreases when RPM rises — pump failing or excessive bearing clearance.
rules.recommendation: Oil pressure should INCREASE with RPM. If pressure drops or stays low at higher RPM, the oil pump cannot keep up with increased bearing flow demand. This indicates worn bearings, failing pump, or oil cavitation.
rules.technicalNotes: Oil pump output = displacement × RPM. If bearings are worn, increased clearance at hot idle → low pressure. Pump wear also reduces output.

rules.name: Engine Oil Overtemperature
rules.description: Oil temperature exceeds 130°C — oil breakdown accelerates rapidly.
rules.recommendation: Oil temperature above 130°C causes rapid oxidation and viscosity breakdown. Oil life halves for every 10°C above 100°C. Reduce load, check oil cooler, and consider an oil change with correct-grade synthetic.
rules.technicalNotes: Arrhenius rule: chemical reaction rate doubles per 10°C. Conventional oil: max 120°C. Full synthetic: max 150°C. Beyond these, additive packages break down.

rules.name: Oil Fuel Dilution from Rich Running
rules.description: Persistent rich running likely diluting engine oil with unburned fuel.
rules.recommendation: Sustained rich running washes unburned fuel past piston rings into crankcase, diluting oil. This reduces oil viscosity and lubrication capacity. Fix the rich condition and change oil promptly.
rules.technicalNotes: Fuel dilution > 5% by volume significantly reduces oil film thickness. Check dipstick for fuel smell. GDI engines prone to fuel dilution during extended cold cranking.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Critical Overheat
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature exceeds 130°C — fluid and clutch damage imminent.
rules.recommendation: STOP towing/heavy load immediately. Pull over and let engine idle to cool transmission. Fluid life halves every 20°F above 175°F (79°C). At 130°C, seals harden, fluid oxidizes, and clutch material degrades rapidly.
rules.technicalNotes: ATF normal operating range: 65-95°C. Caution zone: 95-120°C. Danger zone: >120°C. Fluid change required after any overheating event.

rules.name: Transmission Not Warming Up
rules.description: Transmission fluid temperature still below 40°C after extended driving — temperature sensor fault or extreme conditions.
rules.recommendation: Trans fluid should reach 50°C within 10-15 minutes of driving. Persistent cold reading may indicate temperature sender fault or in very cold climate. Cold ATF has high viscosity causing delayed shifts and increased line pressures.
rules.technicalNotes: ATF viscosity at 0°C is 5-10x higher than at 80°C. Most transmissions have cold shift strategies to account for this.

rules.name: Boost Pressure Below Expected
rules.description: Turbo boost pressure failing to reach expected levels — leak or turbo failure.
rules.recommendation: Low boost pressure under load means turbo not building expected pressure. Check for boost leaks in intercooler piping, wastegate stuck open, or turbo bearing failure reducing spool speed.
rules.technicalNotes: Boost leak test: pressurize charge system to 15 psi with smoke. Listen for leaks at intercooler connections, charge pipes, and turbo outlet.

rules.name: Overboost Condition
rules.description: Boost pressure exceeds safe limit — wastegate not controlling pressure.
rules.recommendation: Overboost risks engine damage from excessive cylinder pressure. ECU should activate fuel cut and limp mode. If persisting, wastegate actuator has failed or boost control solenoid is stuck. Reduce throttle immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: Most OEM turbos are designed for 0.5-1.2 bar boost (150-220 kPa absolute). Sustained overboost causes head gasket failure, rod bearing damage, or piston failure.

rules.name: Turbo RPM Implausibly Low Under Load
rules.description: Turbo shaft speed too low for load demand — bearing failure or mechanical restriction.
rules.recommendation: Turbo should spin 80,000-200,000 RPM under load. Low spool speed indicates worn bearings, oil starvation, or exhaust leak before turbine reducing drive energy.
rules.technicalNotes: Check turbo shaft play: radial play > 0.05mm or axial play > 0.03mm indicates bearing failure. Oil feed line blockage causes catastrophic turbo failure.

rules.name: Turbo Oil Starvation Indicators
rules.description: Low oil pressure combined with turbo operation — risk of turbo bearing failure.
rules.recommendation: Turbochargers require minimum 25 psi oil pressure for journal bearing lubrication. Low oil pressure during boost means turbo bearings are at risk of failure. Check oil level and oil feed line immediately.
rules.technicalNotes: Turbo bearings spin at up to 200,000 RPM on an oil film ~0.01mm thick. Any interruption in oil supply causes immediate scoring and eventual seizure.

rules.name: High Engine Load at Closed Throttle
rules.description: Engine load elevated despite throttle near closed — vacuum leak or IAC issue.
rules.recommendation: High load with closed throttle means air is entering the engine through unintended path. Check for vacuum leaks, stuck open IAC valve, or EGR valve stuck open.
rules.technicalNotes: Smoke test the intake system with throttle closed. Any smoke escaping reveals leak location. Common leak points: brake booster hose, PCV valve, intake manifold gaskets.

rules.name: Vehicle Stationary with High RPM
rules.description: Engine revving high while vehicle is stationary — possible transmission neutral or clutch issue.
rules.recommendation: High RPM with zero speed may indicate neutral rev, slipping clutch, or automatic transmission not engaging. If unintentional, check transmission engagement, torque converter, and shift linkage.
rules.technicalNotes: Automatic: torque converter normally allows some RPM rise at stall. RPM > 3000 in drive with no movement = complete loss of forward engagement.

rules.name: Check Engine Light Ignored Long Distance
rules.description: Vehicle driven 500+ km with MIL active — deferred maintenance risk.
rules.recommendation: Check engine light has been on for over 500 km of driving. Even if the vehicle seems to drive normally, the underlying condition may be causing increased emissions, reduced fuel economy, or secondary damage.
rules.technicalNotes: MIL illumination means at least one emission-related DTC is confirmed. Some DTCs are benign (EVAP small leak), others are damaging (misfire, cat damage).

rules.name: DTCs Recently Cleared
rules.description: DTCs were cleared less than 30 minutes ago — monitors have not completed, potential masking of issues.
rules.recommendation: Diagnostic trouble codes were recently cleared. OBD monitors need 1-3 drive cycles to complete. Results may not fully reflect vehicle condition until monitors are complete.
rules.technicalNotes: After clearing DTCs: readiness monitors reset. EVAP monitor may take 3+ days. Catalyst monitor needs specific drive cycle. Continuous monitors (misfire, fuel) run immediately.

rules.name: Engine Load Implausibly High
rules.description: Calculated engine load above 95% at normal driving — sensor error or turbo issue.
rules.recommendation: Load above 95% without wide-open throttle is implausible for NA engines. Check MAF sensor (over-reading), MAP sensor (stuck high), or if turbocharged check for overboosting. Could also indicate calculated load offset in ECU.
rules.technicalNotes: Calculated load = (actual MAF / theoretical max MAF) × 100%. Max MAF depends on displacement and volumetric efficiency.

rules.name: MAP Sensor Stuck at Fixed Value
rules.description: MAP reading unchanging despite RPM and throttle changes — sensor frozen or electrical fault.
rules.recommendation: MAP sensor reading near atmospheric pressure while engine is running suggests sensor is stuck or disconnected from vacuum source. Check MAP hose for disconnection, cracks, or blockage.
rules.technicalNotes: Disconnected MAP reads atmospheric (~101 kPa). Some ECUs detect this via plausibility check against TPS and RPM.

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Stuck High (Rich)
rules.description: Downstream O2 sensor permanently above 0.7V — sensor fault or catalyst poisoning.
rules.recommendation: Post-cat O2 stuck above 0.7V may indicate contaminated sensor reading falsely rich, or catalyst passing excess fuel through. Monitor pre-cat O2 for comparison.
rules.technicalNotes: Post-cat O2 steady at 0.6-0.7V is normal. Stuck above 0.8V indicates sensor bias or catalyst not oxidizing CO/HC.

rules.name: Post-Cat O2 Stuck Low (Lean)
rules.description: Downstream O2 sensor permanently below 0.2V — air leak in exhaust or sensor failure.
rules.recommendation: Post-cat O2 stuck lean may indicate exhaust leak after catalyst introducing ambient air to sensor, or sensor open circuit. Check exhaust for leaks between cat and sensor location.
rules.technicalNotes: Exhaust leak after cat is common cause of false lean post-cat reading. Listen for ticking sound on cold start. Check for rust at flex pipe or flange joints.

rules.name: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire count elevated on cylinder 2 — coil, plug, or injector for that cylinder.
rules.recommendation: Isolated cylinder 2 misfire. Use coil swap test to determine if coil, plug, or injector. Swap coil from cyl 2 to cyl 3 — if misfire moves to cyl 3, replace coil.
rules.technicalNotes: Coil-on-plug: swap test is definitive. For wasted-spark: each coil fires companion cylinders (1-4, 2-3 on 4-cyl).

rules.name: Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire count elevated on cylinder 3.
rules.recommendation: Isolated cylinder 3 misfire. Perform coil swap test. On 4-cylinder engines, cylinder 3 is often hardest to access — check for heat-related coil failure.
rules.technicalNotes: On many inline-4 engines, cylinders 2 and 3 are hottest due to proximity. Coil failures more common on these cylinders.

rules.name: Cylinder 4 Misfire Detected
rules.description: Misfire count elevated on cylinder 4.
rules.recommendation: Isolated cylinder 4 misfire. Perform coil swap to isolate. On transverse-mounted engines, cylinder 4 is typically closest to the firewall — check for heat damage to wiring.
rules.technicalNotes: Cylinder number convention varies by manufacturer. Always verify firing order for the specific engine.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Sensor Reading Implausible
rules.description: Oil pressure reading above 700 kPa or stuck at zero — sensor fault likely.
rules.recommendation: Normal oil pressure range is 100-500 kPa depending on RPM and temperature. Reading above 700 kPa suggests sensor short to voltage rail or incorrect sensor calibration.
rules.technicalNotes: Oil pressure sensors come in gauge (relative) and absolute types. Incorrect replacement can read 100 kPa high. Verify part number matches application.

rules.name: Coolant and Oil Temperature Divergence
rules.description: Oil temperature 40°C hotter than coolant — oil cooler failure or extreme friction.
rules.recommendation: Normal oil temp is 10-20°C above coolant temp. 40°C+ divergence indicates oil cooler failure, excessive internal engine friction, or oil sump insulation issue. Check oil cooler flow and engine bearing condition.
rules.technicalNotes: Many engines use a plate-type oil cooler integrated with the oil filter housing. Internal blockage prevents heat transfer to coolant circuit.

rules.name: Ambient Temp and IAT Cold Start Agreement
rules.description: Ambient temperature and intake air temperature should agree at cold start — disagreement = sensor fault.

conditions.description: IAT unusually high for cold start

rules.recommendation: At cold start, IAT should be within 10-15°C of ambient temperature. Large disagreement early in drive suggests IAT sensor is located too close to exhaust or is reading incorrectly.
rules.technicalNotes: Heat soak IAT on restart is normal (engine bay retains heat). But brand new cold start after hours of parking should show IAT ≈ ambient.

rules.name: Sustained High Load Thermal Risk
rules.description: Engine load above 80% for extended period — increased thermal stress on all components.
rules.recommendation: Sustained high load operation (towing, hill climbing) stresses cooling, oil, and transmission systems. Monitor temperature gauges closely. Allow engine to idle 30 seconds before shutdown to prevent turbo heat soak (if turbocharged).
rules.technicalNotes: High load + high ambient temp is the most demanding condition for a cooling system. Ensure coolant level is correct and system is bled of air.

rules.name: EGR Position Error Oscillating
rules.description: EGR error fluctuating — valve hunting or feedback sensor intermittent.
rules.recommendation: EGR position error should be near zero during steady-state operation. Oscillating error indicates carbon buildup causing sticking, or EGR position sensor providing intermittent signal.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR valves in diesel engines accumulate more carbon than gasoline. Ultrasonic cleaning is effective for recovery.

rules.name: Engine Over-Revving
rules.description: RPM exceeds 6500 — mechanical overrev risk.
rules.recommendation: Most production engines have rev limiters at 6000-7000 RPM. Exceeding this causes valve float, piston-to-valve contact risk, and excessive bearing loads. If RPM is exceeding rev limiter, ECU fuel cut may be malfunctioning.
rules.technicalNotes: Rev limiter typically works via fuel cut. Some use ignition cut. Over-rev on downshift (money-shift) bypasses ECU protection.

rules.name: Vehicle Speed Sensor Plausibility Issue
rules.description: Vehicle speed reading implausibly high or inconsistent with RPM-gear ratio.
rules.recommendation: Most passenger vehicles cannot exceed 200 km/h. Implausible speed reading suggests VSS (vehicle speed sensor) malfunction, incorrect tire size programming, or damaged tone ring.
rules.technicalNotes: VSS typically uses reluctor ring on transmission output shaft. Damaged teeth cause erratic or doubled speed readings.

rules.name: Transmission Stuck in Low Gear
rules.description: High RPM at moderate speed indicates transmission not upshifting — limp mode or solenoid failure.
rules.recommendation: High RPM at moderate speed with light throttle means transmission is stuck in low gear. Check for transmission DTCs. Common causes: shift solenoid failure, low trans fluid, or ECU commanded limp mode.
rules.technicalNotes: Limp mode typically locks transmission in 2nd or 3rd gear for protection. Check for corresponding engine DTCs that may trigger limp mode.

rules.name: Voltage Fluctuating at Idle
rules.description: ECU voltage fluctuating more than 0.5V at idle — alternator diode failure or loose connection.
rules.recommendation: Voltage should be stable at 13.8-14.4V. Fluctuating voltage at idle suggests alternator diode failure (causing AC ripple), loose belt, or intermittent wiring connection. Check alternator output with multimeter and oscilloscope.
rules.technicalNotes: AC ripple test: place multimeter on AC scale at battery terminals with engine running. > 0.5V AC indicates diode failure in alternator rectifier bridge.

rules.name: Possible Coolant Loss Pattern
rules.description: ECT running progressively higher over time despite thermostat opening — possible coolant loss.
rules.recommendation: ECT above 100°C while driving with adequate airflow suggests coolant level may be low, creating air pockets in cooling system. Check coolant reservoir level. Air in system prevents proper heat transfer at water pump.
rules.estimatedRepairTime: 30 min check + repair if leak found
rules.technicalNotes: Low coolant creates air pocket at water pump, reducing flow by up to 80%. Head gasket leak can cause internal coolant loss with no visible external leak.

rules.name: Lean Condition Only at Wide Open Throttle
rules.description: Engine goes lean at WOT — fuel delivery cannot keep up with demand.

conditions.description: STFT B1 high lean at WOT

rules.recommendation: Lean only at WOT while normal at part load indicates fuel delivery capacity insufficient. Fuel pump weak (not reaching max pressure), fuel filter restricted, or injectors at maximum duty cycle. This causes power loss and potential engine damage.
rules.technicalNotes: WOT fuel demand is 3-5x idle demand. Weak pump may supply adequate pressure at idle but cannot maintain pressure at peak flow.

rules.name: Extended Cold Start High Idle
rules.description: Cold fast idle persisting beyond 5 minutes — IAC not reducing idle speed.
rules.recommendation: Cold fast idle (1100-1500 RPM) should decrease to normal 650-800 RPM as ECT rises above 60°C. Extended high idle indicates IAC stuck open, ECT sensor reading lower than actual, or ECU fast idle strategy fault.
rules.technicalNotes: ECU targets higher idle speed when cold for faster catalyst light-off and stable combustion with cold fuel atomization.

rules.name: Excessive Turbo Lag
rules.description: Boost pressure not building despite throttle application — turbo response delayed.
rules.recommendation: At 2500+ RPM with substantial throttle, boost should develop within 1-2 seconds. No boost response suggests turbo not spooling: worn bearings, seized shaft, wastegate stuck open, or exhaust leak before turbo.
rules.technicalNotes: Turbo spool threshold: ~1500-2000 RPM for modern small turbos, ~2500-3500 RPM for larger turbos. VGT/VNT turbos should spool by 1500 RPM.

rules.name: MAF-MAP Disagreement at Cruise
rules.description: MAF-based and MAP-based load calculations disagree significantly — one sensor drifted.

conditions.description: MAP shows high vacuum despite MAF flow

rules.recommendation: MAF says significant airflow but MAP shows high vacuum — one sensor is incorrect. Contaminated MAF over-reads. Restricted MAP hose under-reads. Cross-reference with load and throttle for confirmation.
rules.technicalNotes: On vehicles with both MAF and MAP, the ECU uses MAF for primary fueling and MAP for backup/plausibility. Disagreement usually sets info DTCs.

rules.name: Timing Retard Under Load
rules.description: Timing advance drops significantly under load — knock detection or fuel octane issue.
rules.recommendation: Some timing retard under load is normal (MBT vs knock limit). Excessive retard (below 10° at high load) suggests low octane fuel, carbon deposits creating hot spots, or failing knock sensor reporting false knock.
rules.technicalNotes: Timing retard from knock: ECU pulls 2-5° per knock event, recovering 1° per 200 combustion events. Sustained retard = persistent knock source.

rules.name: Excessive EVAP Purge Flow
rules.description: EVAP purge duty cycle above 80% — charcoal canister heavily loaded or system leak.
rules.recommendation: Very high EVAP purge suggests charcoal canister is heavily loaded with fuel vapors. This can happen after repeated refueling or if vent valve is stuck. May cause temporary rich condition during purge events.
rules.technicalNotes: Canister capacity is limited. Hot weather, frequent refueling, and topping off the tank increase canister loading.

rules.name: Extended Idle Oil Stress
rules.description: Engine idling for extended period — oil degradation from fuel condensation and acid buildup.
rules.recommendation: Prolonged idling (>30 min) causes fuel and moisture condensation in crankcase, increased acid formation, and reduced oil life. If frequent long idling is required (work vehicle), consider shorter oil change intervals.
rules.technicalNotes: GM Oil Life Monitor reduces remaining life during idle: oil temp too low for proper moisture evaporation, blow-by dilutes oil with unburned fuel.

rules.name: Cold Transmission Fluid Shift Quality
rules.description: Transmission shifting at very cold fluid temperature — increased line pressure and delayed shifts.
rules.recommendation: Very cold ATF (below 20°C) has high viscosity causing firm shifts and delayed engagement. This is normal in cold climate. Allow gentle driving for first few minutes to warm fluid. Avoid heavy throttle until fluid reaches 40°C.
rules.technicalNotes: ATF viscosity at -20°C can be 10-20x higher than at 80°C. CVTs are especially sensitive to cold fluid — belt/chain slippage can occur if loaded heavily when cold.

rules.name: Extended Warmup Enrichment Period
rules.description: Fuel system still in enrichment mode after ECT reaches operating temperature.
rules.recommendation: Warmup enrichment should taper off as ECT reaches 80-90°C. Persistent negative LTFT after warmup may indicate ECT sensor reading low (engine thinks it's still cold), leaking injector, or intake pressure leak.
rules.technicalNotes: ECU enrichment factors: cold cranking (+40%), cold running (+15-25%), warm idle (+0-2%). All should normalize at operating ECT.

rules.name: DPF Temperature Excessive
rules.description: DPF temperature above 700°C — active regeneration running hot.
rules.recommendation: DPF temperatures above 700°C during active regeneration may indicate excessive soot loading causing uncontrolled burn. This can damage the DPF substrate. Ensure regen completes fully by maintaining highway speed.
rules.technicalNotes: Controlled active regen: 550-650°C. Uncontrolled soot burn: 700-1000°C. Cordierite DPF substrate damage threshold: ~1200°C. SiC DPF: ~1600°C.

rules.name: Catalyst Monitor Incomplete
rules.description: Catalyst efficiency monitoring has not completed — readiness flag not set.
rules.recommendation: Catalyst monitor requires specific drive cycle conditions to complete: highway cruise at 60-100 km/h for 5+ minutes after warmup. Short trips may not satisfy monitor enable criteria.
rules.technicalNotes: Catalyst monitor enable: ECT > 70°C, MAF within range, steady cruise for 2+ minutes. Some OEMs require specific RPM-speed patterns.

rules.name: High RPM with Elevated Oil Temperature
rules.description: Sustained high RPM operation with oil temperature above normal — accelerated wear.
rules.recommendation: High RPM generates more friction heat, raising oil temperature. If sustained, this accelerates oil breakdown and bearing wear. Consider synthetic oil rated for higher temperatures if this driving pattern is frequent.
rules.technicalNotes: Bearing loads increase with square of RPM. At 6000 RPM, bearing load is 4x that at 3000 RPM. Oil film thickness must maintain minimum 0.005mm.

rules.name: Sluggish Throttle Response
rules.description: Throttle opening detected but load/RPM response is delayed — carbon or sensor issue.
rules.recommendation: Throttle opening without proportional load increase indicates intake restriction, clogged air filter, carbon buildup on intake valves (GDI engines), or electronic throttle body response delay.
rules.technicalNotes: GDI engines accumulate carbon on intake valve backsides since fuel doesn't wash them. Walnut shell blast is the standard cleaning method.

rules.name: Excessive EGR Flow at High Load
rules.description: EGR commanded too high under load — reduces power and increases smoke (diesel) or misfire (gasoline).
rules.recommendation: EGR should reduce at high load to maintain power and combustion quality. Excessive EGR under load causes misfires on gasoline engines and excessive soot on diesels. Check EGR valve position sensor calibration and ECU control strategy.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR typically maxes at 15-25% at cruise, reduces to 0% at WOT and idle. Above 30% at load dilutes charge excessively.

rules.name: EGR System Inactive Despite No Error
rules.description: EGR commanded at 0% during cruise when it should be active — possible delete or malfunction.
rules.recommendation: EGR should be active during warm cruise conditions to reduce NOx emissions. Zero EGR at cruise may indicate EGR system has been disabled (aftermarket tune) or solenoid is not receiving signal.
rules.technicalNotes: EGR reduces combustion temperature by 100-200°C, cutting NOx by 50-70%. Modern SCR systems supplement or replace EGR in some diesel applications.

rules.name: Coolant Temperature Oscillating
rules.description: ECT cycling up and down by more than 10°C — thermostat flutter or air pocket in system.

conditions.description: ECT in operating range but cycling

rules.recommendation: ECT should stabilize within ±3°C at operating temperature. Wider oscillation indicates thermostat opening/closing erratically, air pocket passing through sensor location, or water pump cavitation.
rules.technicalNotes: Bleed cooling system after any coolant work. Air pockets cause localized hot spots and sensor oscillation. Radiator cap maintains 0.9-1.1 bar to raise boiling point.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Normal at Cold Start Only
rules.description: Oil pressure adequate cold but drops excessively when hot — viscosity loss or bearing wear.
rules.recommendation: Oil pressure drops when hot because oil viscosity decreases with temperature. Excessive drop indicates oil has lost viscosity (fuel dilution, overextended change interval) or bearing clearances have increased from wear.
rules.estimatedRepairTime: 30 min (oil change) or more if bearing issue
rules.technicalNotes: 5W-30 at 100°C: ~10 cSt. At 120°C: ~7 cSt. Fuel-diluted oil loses 30-50% viscosity. Check dipstick for fuel smell and elevated oil level.

rules.name: Transmission Slip Indicator
rules.description: RPM rising faster than vehicle speed proportionally — clutch pack or torque converter slip.
rules.recommendation: RPM rising disproportionate to speed indicates internal slippage. For automatic: worn clutch packs or low fluid level. For manual: worn clutch disc. For CVT: belt/chain slip. Check fluid level and condition first.
rules.technicalNotes: Burnt smell in ATF is a definitive sign of clutch material degradation. Dark brown/black fluid with metallic particles = internal damage.

rules.name: Misfires During Cold Start Only
rules.description: Misfires present in first 2 minutes but clear after warmup — ignition or fueling issue when cold.
rules.recommendation: Cold-only misfires suggest spark plug gap too wide (harder to fire in cold dense charge), weak ignition coil (marginal when cold), or injector with poor spray pattern (fuel atomization poor when cold).
rules.technicalNotes: Cold dense air requires higher spark energy. Worn plugs with 1.3mm+ gap may fire fine when warm but misfire when cold. Check plug gap against specification (typically 0.8-1.1mm).

rules.name: ECU Voltage Affecting Ignition
rules.description: Low system voltage reducing ignition coil charge time — misfires under load.
rules.recommendation: Low voltage reduces coil dwell time, producing weaker spark. This causes misfires especially under load. Fix voltage issue first (alternator, battery) — misfires may resolve without ignition system repair.
rules.technicalNotes: Coil energy = 0.5 × L × I². Dwell time at 14V: ~2.5ms. At 11V: ~3.5ms needed for same energy but ECU may not extend dwell sufficiently.

rules.name: Fuel System Open Loop Rich
rules.description: Fuel system status indicating open loop rich operation — power enrichment or failure mode.
rules.recommendation: Open loop enrichment at part throttle is abnormal. Power enrichment should only activate at WOT (>80% TPS). Possible stuck WOT signal, TPS calibration error, or ECU in fuel enrichment failsafe.
rules.technicalNotes: Fuel system status values: 1=OL, 2=CL, 4=OL-drive, 8=OL-fault. Status 4 at part throttle = investigate TPS and enrichment triggers.

rules.name: Extreme Ambient Temperature Operation
rules.description: Ambient temperature above 45°C — all cooling systems under maximum stress.
rules.recommendation: Extreme ambient heat reduces cooling system effectiveness. AC condenser adds heat load to engine radiator. Monitor all temps closely. Avoid sustained maximum load. Consider reducing AC to reduce engine heat load if temps climb.
rules.technicalNotes: Cooling system is designed for maximum 50°C ambient at full load. At 45°C+ ambient, only 5°C margin remains. AC condenser rejects 5-15 kW of heat directly in front of radiator.

rules.name: STFT Bank 2 Oscillation
rules.description: Short-term fuel trim on Bank 2 oscillating excessively — Bank 2 O2 feedback instability.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 STFT oscillating independently of Bank 1 indicates Bank 2 specific O2 sensor response issue. Check Bank 2 upstream O2 sensor for slow response time and exhaust leaks on Bank 2 manifold.
rules.technicalNotes: Use bidirectional scan tool to compare cross-counts (lean-rich transitions per second) between Bank 1 and Bank 2 upstream O2 sensors. Should be similar.

rules.name: Fuel Pressure Fluctuating
rules.description: Fuel rail pressure swinging more than expected — pump pulsation or regulator issue.
rules.recommendation: Fuel pressure should be stable ±10 kPa. Wide fluctuations suggest weak fuel pump, clogged filter causing intermittent restriction, or fuel pressure regulator diaphragm fatigue.
rules.technicalNotes: Test fuel pump volume: should deliver 750-1000 ml/min for port injection. If flow is adequate but pressure is low, check for restricted return line.

rules.name: Misfires Under Load Only
rules.description: Misfires appear only under acceleration or load — compression or spark energy issue.
rules.recommendation: Misfires only under load suggest insufficient spark energy at higher cylinder pressures. Worn spark plugs, cracked coil insulation (breaks down under load), or low compression on affected cylinder. Compression test is diagnostic.
rules.technicalNotes: Under load, cylinder pressure reaches 12-15 bar, requiring 30-40 kV to fire. At idle, only 10-15 kV needed. Marginal ignition components fail at high pressure first.

rules.name: Voltage Sag Under Electrical Load
rules.description: System voltage drops significantly when electrical loads are applied — alternator capacity marginal.
rules.recommendation: Voltage sag at idle with electrical loads (headlights, heated seats, blower) indicates alternator output marginal at low RPM. Most alternators produce lower current below 1000 RPM. Ensure idle speed is correct and belt is tight.
rules.technicalNotes: Alternator output: ~40% of rated current at idle (600 RPM), full output at 2500+ RPM. 120A alternator produces only ~50A at idle.

rules.name: MAF Sensor Degradation Pattern
rules.description: MAF reporting lower than expected for RPM and throttle position — contamination reducing sensitivity.
rules.recommendation: Low MAF reading with positive LTFT compensation strongly suggests contaminated MAF sensor. Clean with MAF-specific cleaner spray (never touch the element). This is a common maintenance item every 30,000-50,000 km.
rules.technicalNotes: Hot-wire MAF: oil mist from oiled air filters (K&N) contaminates sensing element. Hot-film MAF is less susceptible. Use only dedicated MAF cleaner, not carb cleaner or brake cleaner.

rules.name: O2 Sensor Slow Response Time
rules.description: Upstream O2 transitions between lean and rich too slowly — aging sensor.

conditions.description: O2 spending too much time in transition zone

rules.recommendation: A healthy O2 sensor transitions from lean to rich in under 100ms. Slow-responding sensors spend excessive time in the 0.3-0.6V transition zone, indicating aging. Replace preventively every 100,000-150,000 km.
rules.technicalNotes: O2 sensor response time test: monitor voltage at 2500 RPM. Lean-to-rich should switch in < 100ms. Rich-to-lean < 120ms. Slow = contaminated or aged.

rules.name: DPF Ash Accumulation Long Term
rules.description: DPF baseline pressure gradually rising over time — ash from oil combustion accumulating.

conditions.description: DPF diff pressure moderately elevated baseline

rules.recommendation: Unlike soot, ash cannot be burned off during regeneration. Ash from engine oil accumulates over 100,000-200,000 km, gradually reducing DPF capacity. Professional DPF cleaning (removal + pneumatic/thermal cleaning) restores capacity.
rules.technicalNotes: Each liter of oil consumed deposits ~15-20g of ash in DPF. Low-SAPS oil (ACEA C3/C4) produces less ash. DPF capacity is typically 3-5 liters, holds 30-80g ash.

rules.name: Electronic Throttle Actuator Lag
rules.description: Slow throttle response in drive-by-wire system — motor or circuit resistance issue.
rules.recommendation: Significant gap between pedal position and throttle plate position indicates actuator motor struggling. Carbon buildup creating friction, failing motor, or ECU in reduced power mode (limp). Clear codes and try throttle relearn first.
rules.technicalNotes: Drive-by-wire: APP sensor → ECU → throttle motor → TPS feedback. Discrepancy triggers reduced power mode. Some vehicles need bi-directional adapter for throttle learn.

rules.name: Bank 2 Downstream O2 Actively Switching
rules.description: Bank 2 post-cat O2 showing upstream-like switching pattern — bank 2 catalyst failing.

conditions.description: O2 B2S2 voltage outside stable range

rules.recommendation: Bank 2 downstream O2 switching like an upstream sensor means Bank 2 catalyst has lost oxygen storage capacity. Separate from Bank 1 catalyst issue. Replacement required.
rules.technicalNotes: V-engines often have separate catalysts per bank. One bank can fail while other remains functional. Compare B1S2 and B2S2 patterns to confirm.

rules.name: Oil Pressure Spike Detected
rules.description: Oil pressure jumps abnormally high — possible oil passage blockage or stuck relief valve.
rules.recommendation: Very high oil pressure at moderate RPM indicates oil pump relief valve stuck closed or oil passage blockage. Excessive pressure can blow gaskets and seals. Use correct viscosity oil and check relief valve operation.
rules.technicalNotes: Oil pump relief valve opens at 400-500 kPa to prevent over-pressurization. Stuck closed: usually contamination or debris. Using too-thick oil (e.g., 20W-50 in 5W-30 engine) also raises pressure excessively.

rules.name: Wastegate Control Issue
rules.description: Boost pressure not matching expected levels despite RPM and throttle — wastegate position incorrect.

conditions.description: Boost lower than expected under load

rules.recommendation: At high RPM and WOT, boost should reach target pressure. Partial boost suggests wastegate is partially stuck open, boost control solenoid is weak, or vacuum/pressure lines to wastegate actuator are leaking.
rules.technicalNotes: Internal wastegate: test by disconnecting actuator vacuum line — should build max boost. External wastegate: check spring preload. Electronic wastegate: check actuator motor and position sensor.

rules.name: Excessive Crankcase Pressure Indicator
rules.description: Oil filler cap pushes off or excessive blow-by — piston ring or PCV issue.
rules.recommendation: Excessive blow-by gases pressurize crankcase, force oil past seals, and contaminate intake via PCV. Check for stuck PCV valve, replace if rattling stops. If PCV is OK, compression test needed to evaluate ring seal.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal blow-by: 1-3% of cylinder volume at idle. Worn rings: 5-10% or more. Blue smoke from exhaust is confirmatory. Oil consumption > 1L per 1000 km is excessive.

rules.name: Harsh Mechanical Shifting
rules.description: Transmission fluid extremely hot combined with high RPM — harsh engagement likely.
rules.recommendation: Hot transmission fluid combined with elevated RPM shifts causes clutch band wear. High temp reduces fluid friction modifier effectiveness. Check ATF level (hot check per manual), consider ATF drain and refill.
rules.technicalNotes: ATF change: drain and refill is safer than flush for high-mileage transmissions. Replace 4-5 quarts at a time. Repeat after 500 km for a gradual exchange.

rules.name: Both Banks Lean — System-Wide Issue
rules.description: Both banks showing positive fuel trims — system-wide lean condition not bank-specific.
rules.recommendation: Both banks lean rules out bank-specific causes (intake gasket, O2 sensor). Focus on shared system: low fuel pressure, contaminated MAF reading low, large vacuum leak at throttle body gasket or brake booster.
rules.technicalNotes: On V-engines, both banks lean = pre-throttle body issue. Single bank lean = post-throttle body issue. This distinction eliminates many possible causes.

rules.name: Both Banks Rich — System-Wide Issue
rules.description: Both banks showing negative fuel trims — system-wide rich condition.
rules.recommendation: Both banks rich = shared cause: high fuel pressure, MAF over-reading, leaking fuel pressure regulator, EVAP purge valve stuck open flooding intake with fuel vapors. Not individual injector or bank-specific issue.
rules.technicalNotes: Stuck-open purge valve test: disconnect EVAP purge line at intake — if rich condition improves immediately, purge valve is stuck open.

rules.name: Catalyst at Temperature But Inefficient
rules.description: Catalyst temperature is normal but downstream O2 indicates poor efficiency — catalyst degraded.

conditions.description: Post-cat O2 not in ideal range

rules.recommendation: Catalyst at operating temperature but not converting efficiently indicates chemical degradation of the washcoat. Causes: fuel contamination (lead, sulfur), excessive thermal cycling, or natural aging. Replace catalyst.
rules.technicalNotes: Three-way catalyst: Pt/Pd oxidizes CO+HC, Rh reduces NOx. Washcoat surface area degrades through sintering (thermal aging) and chemical poisoning.

rules.name: IAT Rising in Traffic — Heat Soak
rules.description: Intake air temperature steadily increasing while stopped in traffic — underhood heat soak.
rules.recommendation: When stationary and engine is hot, underhood temperature rises and heats intake ducting. This is normal but reduces power by ~3% per 10°C above ambient. Ensure air filter box draws from fender well, not engine bay.
rules.technicalNotes: Ram air effect at 100 km/h reduces IAT by 10-15°C vs stationary. Traffic causes IAT to approach ECT due to radiation and conduction from engine.

rules.name: Fresh Start — Monitors Resetting
rules.description: Engine running less than 60 seconds — all readings may be in transient state.
rules.recommendation: Engine has just started. Fuel trims are in open loop, O2 sensors are warming up, and ECT has not stabilized. Allow 2-3 minutes of running before interpreting diagnostic data for accuracy.
rules.technicalNotes: First 30s: open loop enrichment, O2 heaters activating. 30-90s: transition to closed loop. 90s+: normal feedback operation. LTFT not updated during open loop.

rules.name: High Gear at Low Speed — Lugging
rules.description: Vehicle in high gear at low speed — engine lugging risks bearing and drivetrain stress.
rules.recommendation: Lugging the engine (high gear/low RPM under load) creates excessive combustion pressure at low RPM when oil film is weakest. Sustained lugging damages crankshaft, rod bearings, and timing chain. Downshift to maintain 1500+ RPM.
rules.technicalNotes: Below 1000 RPM, oil pump flow is minimal and hydrodynamic bearing film is thinnest. Engine knock is most likely at low RPM/high load. Diesels are more tolerant than gasoline engines.

rules.name: O2 Heater Circuit Problem Indicated
rules.description: O2 sensor still at bias voltage after 60+ seconds — heater not activating.
rules.recommendation: O2 heater should reach operating temperature within 30-60 seconds. Still at bias voltage after 60s means heater circuit failure. Check O2 heater fuse, relay, and wiring. ECU will remain in open loop affecting fuel economy.
rules.technicalNotes: O2 heater resistance: 4-15 ohms typical. Open circuit (infinity) = broken heater element. Short (< 2 ohms) = internal fault. Test with sensor disconnected.

rules.name: Bank 2 LTFT Extremely Positive
rules.description: Bank 2 long-term fuel trim above 20% — severe lean condition on Bank 2.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 LTFT above 20% is approaching ECU compensation limit. Imminent P0174 code. On V-engines: check lower intake manifold gasket on Bank 2, injectors bank 2 clogged, or exhaust leak before B2 O2 sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: GM 3.4L/3.8L V6 notorious for lower intake manifold gasket failure causing bank-specific lean codes. Ford 4.6L: intake manifold runner control (IMRC) gaskets.

rules.name: Bank 2 LTFT Extremely Negative
rules.description: Bank 2 long-term fuel trim below -20% — severe rich condition on Bank 2.
rules.recommendation: Bank 2 LTFT below -20% means severe rich condition. Leaking injector on Bank 2, or Bank 2 O2 sensor reporting false lean (causing ECU to add fuel). Disconnect B2 O2 to see if LTFT normalizes.
rules.technicalNotes: Leaking injector test: measure fuel pressure drop after shutdown. >50 kPa drop in 5 minutes = leaking injector. Identify which bank by clamping fuel rails alternately.

rules.name: Transmission Fluid Degradation Pattern
rules.description: Transmission running consistently above 100°C — accelerated fluid degradation.
rules.recommendation: ATF above 100°C for sustained periods degrades faster. Fluid life halves for every 10°C above 80°C ideal. Check trans cooler lines for blockage, consider auxiliary trans cooler if towing frequently.
rules.technicalNotes: Dexron VI rated for 100,000 km at 80°C. At 100°C: ~50,000 km. At 120°C: ~25,000 km. Fluid color: red/pink = good, brown = degraded, black = burnt.

rules.name: Oil Temperature Below Operating Range
rules.description: Oil temperature below 70°C after extended driving — oil not reaching efficiency temperature.
rules.recommendation: Oil needs to reach 80-100°C to evaporate moisture and fuel condensation. Persistently cold oil ('short trip syndrome') leads to sludge formation. Consider extended highway driving periodically or shorter oil change intervals.
rules.technicalNotes: Oil temperature always lags coolant temperature. Full-synthetic oil resists sludge formation better when oil temp is chronically low.

rules.name: MAP-Barometric Pressure Disagreement
rules.description: MAP reading higher than barometric pressure at idle — physically impossible without forced induction.
rules.recommendation: On naturally aspirated engines, MAP cannot exceed barometric pressure at idle. Reading above atmospheric indicates MAP sensor offset, disconnected vacuum line, or sensor malfunction.
rules.technicalNotes: MAP at idle should be 25-50 kPa for healthy NA engine (16-21 inHg vacuum). Reading near atmospheric = zero vacuum = restricted exhaust or massive air leak.

rules.name: Excessive Cranking Voltage Drop
rules.description: System voltage below 10V during cranking indication — battery capacity insufficient.
rules.recommendation: Minimum cranking voltage should stay above 10.5V. Below 10V suggests battery has insufficient CCA (cold cranking amps), corroded terminals adding resistance, or starter motor drawing excessive current.
rules.technicalNotes: Battery CCA degrades ~5% per year. A 5-year battery has ~75% original CCA. Voltage drop test: >0.5V at battery cable = replace cable.

rules.name: Slow Catalyst Warmup
rules.description: Catalyst temperature rising slowly — delayed light-off increases cold start emissions.
rules.recommendation: Catalyst should reach 200°C within 2-3 minutes. Slow warmup indicates exhaust heat loss (leak, long exhaust manifold), or ECU not using close-coupled secondary air injection for fast warmup.
rules.technicalNotes: Cold start emissions are 70-80% of total drive cycle emissions. ECU strategies to speed light-off: retarded ignition, secondary air, high idle. Pre-cat close to engine heats fastest.

rules.name: Both Coolant and Intake Air Hot
rules.description: ECT and IAT both elevated — engine bay thermal saturation, all systems stressed.
rules.recommendation: Both coolant and intake air running hot creates compound stress: reduced power from hot intake air AND risk of overheating. This occurs in traffic on hot days. Reduce AC load if possible and find a way to get the vehicle moving for airflow.
rules.technicalNotes: Engine bay temperature can exceed 120°C in traffic. Underhood components (rubber hoses, electrical connectors) degrade faster in sustained high-heat environments.

rules.name: High Idle Load with AC Compressor
rules.description: Engine load elevated at idle — AC compressor adding significant parasitic load.
rules.recommendation: AC compressor adds 3-7 HP load at idle. High load reading at idle with AC is normal but if RPM drops below 600 or stalls, IAC valve not compensating. Clean throttle body and check idle up solenoid.
rules.technicalNotes: AC compressor clutch engagement: momentary 50-100 RPM drop is normal. ECU should compensate within 0.5s. Persistent drop = IAC response issue.

rules.name: Fuel Trim Affected by Altitude
rules.description: Positive fuel trims correlating with low barometric pressure — altitude compensation.
rules.recommendation: At altitude, lower air density means less oxygen per volume. ECU may show slight positive LTFT as it compensates. This is normal up to +5-8%. Also expect slight power loss (~3% per 300m elevation).
rules.technicalNotes: Standard atmosphere: 101.3 kPa at sea level. Denver (~1600m): ~83 kPa. Mexico City (~2250m): ~76 kPa. NA engines lose ~3% power per 300m. Turbo engines compensate by running higher boost.

rules.name: Speed-RPM Ratio Abnormal
rules.description: Vehicle speed high but RPM low (or vice versa) relative to expected gear ratio — incorrect tire size or VSS issue.
rules.recommendation: Speed-to-RPM ratio depends on tire size, gear ratio, and final drive. Abnormal ratio may indicate wrong tire size (larger tires = faster speed per RPM), VSS calibration error, or torque converter lockup in wrong gear.
rules.technicalNotes: Expected ratio: at 100 km/h, RPM varies by transmission. 5-speed manual: 2500-3000 RPM. 6-speed auto: 1800-2200 RPM. CVT: 1500-2000 RPM.

rules.name: Exhaust Restriction Indicator
rules.description: Engine load high with moderate throttle — possible exhaust restriction reducing breathing.
rules.recommendation: High load at moderate throttle and RPM suggests the engine is working harder than expected to maintain speed. Possible exhaust restriction (collapsed catalyst, crushed pipe) or intake restriction (clogged filter).
rules.technicalNotes: Exhaust backpressure test: at 2500 RPM, measure backpressure with gauge in upstream O2 sensor bung. Should be < 1.5 psi (10 kPa). Higher = restriction.

rules.name: Slow Voltage Recovery After Load
rules.description: System voltage slow to recover after electrical load removed — alternator output regulation degrading.
rules.recommendation: At 1500+ RPM, voltage should reach 13.8-14.5V within seconds of load removal. Sluggish recovery suggests alternator brushes wearing, voltage regulator aging, or battery absorbing excess current (weak battery acting as a sponge).
rules.technicalNotes: Smart charging systems (BMW, Ford) intentionally run lower voltage during deceleration for fuel savings. This is normal behavior, not a fault.

rules.name: EGR Tracking Error at Highway Cruise
rules.description: EGR actual position differs from commanded during steady cruise — valve sticking intermittently.
rules.recommendation: EGR position tracking error at highway cruise indicates intermittent carbon binding. Valve should track within 2-3% of command. Clean EGR valve and inspect passage for carbon accumulation.
rules.technicalNotes: Use intake system cleaner periodically to prevent carbon buildup. Some vehicles have EGR cooler that can also accumulate deposits and restrict flow.

rules.name: MAF Response Lag on Acceleration
rules.description: MAF not responding quickly to throttle changes — contamination reducing hot-wire sensitivity.
rules.recommendation: MAF should respond within milliseconds to airflow changes. Sluggish response causes momentary lean on acceleration (hesitation/stumble). Clean MAF sensor with dedicated MAF cleaner spray.
rules.technicalNotes: MAF response time spec: < 15ms for 10-90% step change. Contaminated hot-wire: thermal mass increases causing sluggish response. Film-type MAF less affected.

rules.name: Coolant Heat Migrating to Intake
rules.description: IAT rising proportional to ECT increase — heat transfer from engine to intake significantly high.
rules.recommendation: Hot engine heating intake air while stationary is normal but indicates inadequate thermal isolation. Check for missing heat shields, intake ducting drawing air from near exhaust manifold, or aftermarket intake system without heat shield.
rules.technicalNotes: Factory air boxes draw from fender well (cold air). Aftermarket intakes often draw from engine bay (hot air). Net power loss despite claimed gains.

rules.name: Fuel Level Reading Fluctuating
rules.description: Fuel level jumping erratically — fuel level sender or wiring intermittent.
rules.recommendation: Fuel level should change smoothly and gradually. Erratic readings indicate worn fuel level sender (rheostat with dead spots), intermittent wiring, or fuel slosh baffles missing allowing fuel to slosh past sensor.
rules.technicalNotes: Most fuel level senders use a float with rheostat. As rheostat wears, it develops dead spots causing the gauge to jump. Module inside tank; often sold with fuel pump assembly.

rules.name: Idle Timing Too Advanced
rules.description: Ignition timing at idle above 20° — unusual idle advance may indicate ECU compensation for another issue.
rules.recommendation: Normal idle timing is 8-15° BTDC for most engines. Above 20° suggests ECU is advancing timing to maintain idle speed, possibly compensating for a vacuum leak or low compression that requires more efficient combustion.
rules.technicalNotes: Some ECUs advance idle timing to compensate for low power conditions rather than increasing airflow. This is a secondary indicator of underlying issue.

rules.name: Excessive Total Idle Time
rules.description: Engine running for extended period at idle — component wear and emissions concern.
rules.recommendation: Extended idling wastes fuel (~0.5-1.0 L/hour), accumulates engine hours without productive use, and increases emissions. Modern engines are designed to be turned off rather than idle for extended periods.
rules.technicalNotes: 1 hour of idling ≈ 30 km of driving in terms of engine wear. Some fleet vehicles have idle-shutdown timers. Start-stop systems were designed specifically to reduce unnecessary idle.

rules.name: Oil Viscosity Breakdown Suspected
rules.description: Oil pressure lower than expected for RPM — oil may have lost viscosity from heat or fuel dilution.
rules.recommendation: At 2000+ RPM with warm oil, pressure should be 200-400 kPa. Consistently lower suggests oil viscosity has dropped — either from age (shear thinning), fuel dilution, or using wrong viscosity grade. Change oil promptly.
rules.technicalNotes: 5W-30 viscosity at 100°C: typically 10-12 cSt. After 15,000 km: may drop to 8-9 cSt. Below 8 cSt: change immediately. Lab oil analysis can confirm viscosity, fuel dilution, and wear metals.

rules.name: Boost Spike on Throttle Lift
rules.description: Momentary boost spike when releasing throttle — bypass/blow-off valve slow to react.
rules.recommendation: When throttle closes during boost, the compressor surge pressure must be relieved by the blow-off valve (atmosphere vent) or bypass valve (recirculated). Slow response causes compressor surge, which is audible as a flutter and stresses turbo bearings.
rules.technicalNotes: Compressor surge occurs when airflow reverses through compressor against closed throttle. Sustained surge causes thrust bearing and compressor wheel damage. Recirculating valve preferred for MAF-equipped cars.

rules.name: Ambient Temperature Sensor Stuck
rules.description: Ambient temperature reading not changing despite driving through different conditions.

conditions.description: Ambient in plausible range but may be stuck

rules.recommendation: If ambient temperature sensor reads a fixed value regardless of conditions, it may be stuck or have a poor connection. While this sensor is informational, some ECUs use it for adaptive fuel strategies.
rules.technicalNotes: Ambient temp sensor is typically in the front bumper area. Exposure to road debris and moisture causes connector corrosion. Reading may be affected by radiator heat at idle.

rules.name: Fuel System Closed Loop Override
rules.description: Fuel system status toggling between open and closed loop unexpectedly.

conditions.description: Not at WOT (should be closed loop)

rules.recommendation: At operating temp and part throttle, fuel should be in closed loop. If toggling to open loop, check O2 sensor heater status, for intermittent O2 signal dropout, or exhaust leak cooling sensor periodically.
rules.technicalNotes: Closed-to-open transitions can be caused by O2 sensor intermittent. Monitor O2 voltage with graphing tool to see if signal drops out momentarily.

rules.name: TPS Sensor Dead Spot Indicated
rules.description: Throttle position sensor may have a worn spot — erratic reading at certain positions.

conditions.description: TPS in common dead spot range

rules.recommendation: Rheostat-type TPS can develop worn spots, causing momentary signal dropout at certain positions. This creates hesitation or surge at specific throttle openings. Replace TPS sensor if symptoms correlate with position.
rules.technicalNotes: Use a graphing scan tool to sweep TPS slowly from 0-100%. Voltage should increase smoothly without dropout. Any flat spot, spike, or dropout = failed sensor.

rules.name: Torque Converter Lockup Shudder
rules.description: RPM oscillation during expected lockup speed — torque converter clutch slipping.
rules.recommendation: Shudder during torque converter lockup is caused by worn TCC friction material or degraded ATF friction modifiers. Try ATF drain-and-refill with correct OEM-spec fluid. If shudder persists, torque converter replacement is needed.
rules.technicalNotes: TCC shudder is the #1 torque converter complaint. Fluid exchange resolves 60-70% of cases. Shudder-fix additives work temporarily. Permanent fix: new converter + fresh ATF.

rules.name: Oil System Operating Normally
rules.description: Oil pressure and temperature within expected ranges — system healthy.
rules.recommendation: Oil system parameters are within normal operating range. Continue regular oil change intervals per manufacturer specification. Monitor for any developing trends.
rules.technicalNotes: Normal oil pressure: 25-65 psi (170-450 kPa) depending on RPM. Normal oil temp: 80-110°C. Both parameters confirm healthy lubrication system.
